essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object. [Clarification Statement: Examples could include an unbalanced force on one side of a ball can make it start moving; and, balanced forces pushing on a box from both sides will not produce any motion at all.] [Assessment Boundary: Assessment is limited to one variable at a time: number, size, or direction of forces. Assessment does not include quantitative force size, only qualitative and relative. Assessment is limited to gravity being addressed as a force that pulls objects down.]

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: pull, push, force, speed, velocity, frictional force, mass, balanced force, unbalanced force, gravity force, magnetic force
Identify forces as pushes or pulls.
Identify forces used to create movement or change in given situations.
Give examples of balanced and unbalanced forces.
Describe the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces acting on a variety of everyday objects.
Identify force as a push or pull by one body on another.
Identify, demonstrate and give examples of push and pulls (ST 1 PS FMS 1).
Describe and demonstrate the forces of push and pull. (ST 3 PS FMS 1).
Identify, with examples, forces at work in everyday situations in the home and community. (ST 3 PS FMS 2).
Describe the visible effects of push and pull on a variety of everyday objects. (ST 4 PS FMS 4).
Compare balanced and unbalanced forces using a Venn Diagram.
Demonstrate ways in which unbalanced forces can cause change in motion (start movement, increase speed, reduce speed, change direction).
Demonstrate the effect of balanced forces on objects.
Conduct an Investigation on the ways in which different forces (e.g. magnetism, static electricity, muscular force, gravitational force) can change the speed and direction of a moving object.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.
Students will read each scenario then draw a line to match.

Draw and write to show how you can use force to affect motion in each way: 1. Make something start moving, 2. Make something stop moving, 3. Make something slow down, 4. Make something speed up, 5. Make something change direction, 6. Make something change shape.
Define the terms: Arc, Motion, Force, Force of gravity, Magnetic force, Pattern, Predict
Identify the different types of motion (ways objects move).
Describe patterns in the environment (natural and human).
Describe the movement of various objects.
Demonstrate they understand that objects can move in various ways.
Compare the different ways objects can move (straight line, zig zag, back and forth, circular).
Explain the various ways objects can move in the environment.
Identify the conditions needed for objects to move in a given situation. (ST 2 PS EN 1)
Identify with examples forces at work in everyday situations in the home and community. (ST 3 PS FMS 2)
Observe objects in motion.
Infer that patterns can be repeated by engaging in scientific investigations.
Classify the motion of objects.
Communicate ideas on patterns and motions with their peers.
Predict what should come next in patterns.
Construct a pendulum and a spiral marble track to observe motion.
Illustrate or draw the various ways objects can move in the environment.
Distinguish between a motion and a pattern.
Demonstrate ways in which motion can be changed. (ST 2 PS FMS 1).
Describe and demonstrate the forces of push and pull (ST 3 PS FMS 1).
Classify different kinds of motion according to what causes the motion (e.g. gravity, magnets) (ST 4 PS FMS 2).
Infer that the position of the sun changes at different times of the day. (ST 2 ESS SS 3).
Outline the relationship between earth, moon and sun, especially how the relationship affects humans. (ST 3 ESS SS 1).
Work safely when participating in various activities.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Work collaboratively in groups.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Literacy Development: Teachers may use a Frayer Four Square to monitor students' understanding of key terminology developed in the lesson.

Practicing Terminology- Jeopardy: Students will engage in a game of jeopardy to assess students' knowledge on defining the terms. Students can work individually or in groups. Write a list of the words in an area where all can view. (pull, push, force, friction, mass, balanced force, unbalanced force, gravity). On an empty wall, create a grid with 16 papers (letter size) (4x4). Each grid will have points ranging from 100 to 400. Under each grid will be 8 definitions and 8 scenarios. Under the paper the teacher will write the definitions as written below. Meanings: 1. Push: the act of moving an object away from oneself. 2. Pull: the act of moving an object towards oneself. 3. Force: is any push or pull on an object. 4. Friction is a force that acts between two objects that are in contact with one another. 5. Mass is the measurement of how much matter is contained in an object. 6. Balanced Force is the acting on an object which does not change the state of rest or uniform motion of it. 7. Unbalanced Force is when the force applied in one direction is greater
Gravity: the force that attracts a body towards the centre of the earth, or towards any other physical body having mass.

Scenarios: 1. I am turning up the volume on my cell phone. Which word describes the force that I used? (what is push?) 2. I am trying to take my book from my teacher. What word best describes the force that is used? (what is pull?) 3. I placed my pencil on the table but it rolled off. (what is an unbalanced force?) 4. I throw my ball up towards the ceiling but it always seems to fall back down. What word best describes the force that pulls the ball down? (What is gravity) 5. I placed my apple on my stack of textbooks and it's not moving. What word best describes this force? (what is a balanced force?) 6. I am pushing my dresser across my bedroom. What am I creating between the floor and the dresser? (what is friction) 7. I can choose to push or pull my grocery cart. What do I use to move the cart? (what is force) 8. I can be heavy or light (what is mass)

The teacher will decide on the arrangement on the grid with the top row being 100 points, 2nd row being 200 points, third row being 300 points, and bottom row being 400 points. When the student is ready to play they will select the points they want added to their total score. If they get the answer or respond correctly the points will be added to their total. If they did not respond correctly the teacher will give the next student or team a chance to respond and if they get it correct, they get the score. Now if no one gets it, then the teacher will reveal the answer and the sheet is placed on the side.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Understanding Force as a Push or Pull

Look at these two pictures. What do they show? (Girls on swings with their dads) Have you ever been on a swing? (Yes/No) Do you like being on a swing? (Yes/No) How does the swing work? What is being pushed? (The swing. / The person on the swing,) What happens when the swing is pushed? (The girl moves forward) What is being pulled?(The swing. / The person on the swing) What happens when the swing is pulled? (The girl goes backward)

In this picture, who was pushed? Who was pulled? How did you know?(pushed: boy in white shirt, orange shirt - forward movement pulled: girl in blue, boys in red - backward movement). In order to play on a swing, forces must be used. Have you ever heard the word force? What is a force? (Write down the students suggestions). A force is any push or pull on an object. A force that changes the direction of an object towards you, would be a pull. On the other hand, if it moves away, it is a push. Just like we observed with the girl on the swing. When a pushing force was applied, she moved forward and when a pulling force was applied, she moved backward. Formative Assessment: Imagine you were sitting in a chair with your eyes closed and someone pushed it. Explain in your own words, what you would feel as you moved? How would that feeling be different if they pulled the chair. Can you use the word force in a sentence that helps you describe that feeling?

Let us listen and watch the video carefully to learn more about forces. As you watch, think about answering these questions: 1) What does it mean to put something in motion? (get it moving) 2) What is needed to create motion? (apply a force) 3) How is a push different from a pull? (push – the object is away from you/ your body; pull – the object is towards you/your body). Click on the link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-lOyW3pEUl0 (2.50 mins). Review on board. A push force is the force that is responsible for an object moving from the state of rest away from the person or thing applying the force. Some examples of push forces are: Pushing the trolley. Pushing the car when it breaks down. Pushing the table from one place to another. Can you think of other examples of push forces? (students share their experiences with push forces. Add good examples to the list of push forces). Let us discuss pull forces. A pull force is the force that is responsible for an object moving from the state of rest but towards the person or thing applying the force.

Some examples of pull forces are: Pulling the curtain. Dragging the box. Opening of the door. Can you think of other examples of pull forces? (students share their experiences with pull forces. Add good examples to the list of pull forces). Look at the exercise below. Complete it using the knowledge acquired from the above information. Look at each picture carefully, place a tick next to the correct answer. Have Fun!!

Push or Pull Sorting Look at each picture. Check whether it is an example of a push or a pull. Retrieved from: https://www.kidsacademy.mobi/printables/push-or-pull-1/ Look at the pictures below. They are all examples of activities we engage in using either a push or a pull.
Can you think of other activities, games or scenarios where we can either push or pull for it to function? Share them with the class.

Forces and Motion You have probably heard the word "force" before in conversations. Here are a few examples: "the rocket had a lot of force to blast off" or "the force of the storm blew the roof off the building." What is force? Force is defined as a push or pull on an object. Retrieved from: https://dribbble.com/shots/3822584-Going-for-a-Ride/attachments/10039979?mode=media When you ride a bike, your foot pushes against the pedal. The push makes the wheels of the bike move. Forces affect how objects move. They may cause motion; they may also slow, stop, or change the direction of motion of an object that is already moving. Since force causes changes in the speed or direction of an object, we can say that forces cause changes in velocity. Avoid Confusing Speed and Velocity Students, sometimes people confuse speed and velocity. They are not the same. Speed is simply how fast you are going. Velocity is not only how fast you are going but, in what direction that you are traveling. Example: Sarah was traveling 10 kms per hour on the street in front of her house. Philp was riding his scooter 15 kms per hour west ward on the same street.

We say that Sarah was travelling with a speed of 10 kms per hour whereas Philip was traveling at a velocity of 15 kms per hour west. Impacts of Force: Today we will work in pairs. I want each pair to get a ball and show ways you can: (a) start the ball moving or set it in motion (b) change its direction (c) slow down or speed it up (increase speed) (d) stop it from moving. Now let us discuss the various ways forces can cause change in motion. Now with your partner you are going to choose another object example toy car, pencil. I also want you to complete the table below while doing this activity. We chose to use a: ________ Table and activity retrieved from: Caribbean Primary Science Level 2

Therefore, it can be interpreted that a push or pull force can do the following actions itself: A push or pull force may make an object move from rest. A push or pull force may change the speed of a moving object. A push or pull force may change the direction of a moving object. A push or pull force on an object or body may change the shape of an object. Examples of everyday activities which require either a push or a pull. With it, life would surely be difficult. 1. Thumb Pins 2. Opening and Closing a Door 3. Pushing a Car 4. Pulling a Cart 5. Inserting and Removing a Plug 6. Water Dispensers 7. Pulling Curtains and Blinds 8. Pushing Furniture 9. Typing 10. Walking 11. Playing Football 12. Dragging a Suitcase Let us now create a toy truck. Can you guess which force can be used to move it?

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X-b9YS-mAIO Link to video - How to make RC Tipper Truck from cardboard // very simple // the crafts crew. Balanced and Unbalanced Forces: You will be given the following block shapes: Place one square block on the left side of your semicircle (moon shaped) block. What did you observe happen to the blocks when the square was placed on one side of the semicircle? (the half moon tipped t one side) Why do you think this happened? (Heavier side) Now place both blocks on either side of the semicircle, what do we observe? (the half moon block levels out)
What do you observe when both square blocks are placed on either side of your moon shaped block? Why do you think this is so?

What game are these two boys playing? (tug of war) Have you ever played this game before? (yes/no) Who wins in a tug of war game? (team/person that is pulled towards the other or falls over) Does the tug of war require a push or a pull force in order to play? (pull) Who is winning the game in the picture? (no one) Why? (Both boys are the same distances from the blue box and are both standing upright.)

Who applied more force in playing the game? (same amount of force was applied by both boys) The forces applied by these two boys are balanced. Balanced force is when two forces are pulling in the opposite direction are equal just like the picture of the two boys playing tug of war. There is no movement when forces are balanced. If two persons are pushing the same box from opposite sides, the box will remain still. In groups now you will demonstrate the effect of a balanced force by engaging in another activity where you and your partner will push a desk or box from the opposite side. Each person on the opposite side. Why didn't the desk move? (with an equal amount of force, then neither of the team will move and it will be a tie)

What type of force prevented the desk from moving - balanced or unbalanced? (Unbalanced) What causes forces to remain balanced? Balanced forces are: Equal in size, Cause no movement, Act in opposite direction. To conclude this activity we now will listen and view the book entitled : Balancing Act by Ellen Walsh. I want you to listen carefully so you can answer the following questions: 1) What is a teeter totter? (a board over a center that can move up & down) 2) How did the mice balance the teeter totter? (one mouse on each side) 3) If they stand in exactly the same place on each side, what do we know must be true for the teeter totter to balance? (each mouse must weigh the same) 4) Will the teeter totter balance if the mice are at different distances from the center of the teeter totter? (no, the closer one will push upward)

5) Why does the addition of a salamander on one side cause an unbalanced tetter totter? (more weight on one side pushes down) Video reading here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H22Gv85IS_Q (4:32 min)

Let us now play our own tug of war game. There will be two teams. I will need eight students to assist, three students on team A and five students on Team B. (Materials: rope, chalk, ruler). [Mathematics: measurement link in preparing for the tug of war activity] There is an ‘X’ and two lines on the floor/ground. Each team stands behind a line. The team that wins the game pulls the other team over the ‘X’ that is on the ground. Retrieved from https://ssimphal.nic.in/images/3012.jpg Who won our tug of war game? (Team A/B) Why did this team win? (They pulled harder) Did they apply the same amount of force? (The team that won applied more force).

In our tug of war game both teams applied forces in opposite directions but the same amount of force was not applied. This is an example of unbalanced forces. Unbalanced forces means that the force applied in one direction is greater than the force applied in the opposite direction. When unbalanced forces are acting on an object, there is a change in speed and/or direction. Let’s see if we can spot at least two everyday examples of balanced and unbalanced forces in the video that you are about to watch. (tug of war and arm wrestling) Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yzCNniwuw (5:53 mins) Comparing the Balanced and Unbalanced Forces As a review, the teacher can explain the following Venn diagram comparison. Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_-yzCNniwuw

Describing Forces Activity [Students will be placed in 5/6 groups for this activity] In your groups you have been given a card. Each card has a picture that shows the actions of a force on an object. Using the questions provided on the worksheet attached, discuss within your group what is happening in your picture with regard to forces. You will then present your findings to the rest of the class (The group will select a representative who will write the information and draw pictures where necessary, on a Bristol board sheet. All members will brainstorm what is happening in the picture. The selected representative will read out the information to the class) Group 1 – Picture of a mango hanging from a tree branch / a book lying on a desk. Retrieved from: https://encrypted-tbn0.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcQo6dky73fTXb0lmzjbOG8TIqeht0GuSFA92w&usqp=CAU Retrieved from: https://encrypted-tbn0.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcSWRB1BvOY1qgDa5-Z0ocTdQGLQD18XrXk0IQ&usqp=CAU

Let us remember the following things about forces: 
• Forces act in pairs.
• Forces act in a particular direction.
• Forces usually cannot be seen, but their effects can.

Things can move when more force is applied in one direction than another. Movement is caused by unbalanced forces. Forces can make objects move in different ways.

We want to answer the question: How do forces change motion?
An Example from Cricket

Let us consider three friends playing with a ball and a cricket bat.

Picture retrieved from: https://depositphotos.com/photos/sport-of-cricket.html and modified.

Joshua thinks that forces act on the cricket ball you see on the ground only when Collin hits the ball. Jada thinks that there are forces acting on the ball as it lays still on the ground as well as when Collin hits it with the cricket bat.

Who do you think has the best idea about the forces acting on the ball? (Joshua/Jada)

Explain why you think this person has the best idea?

Students write down their predictions in complete sentences. Share your ideas with your classmates on your table/in your group. Who does your group think has the best idea and why? Let us share with the entire class.

When Collin hits the ball with a bat, he is applying a force, a hard push against the ball, the ball may move along the ground, or it can fly upwards.

Let us try hitting a ball with a bat to see if we can get the ball to change its motion. We will hit the ball first on the pavement/concrete and then on the grass/ carpet. We will record what we did in our groups and how the ball moved. (Materials: balls, bat, grass/carpet, recording table or chart).

Retrieved from: https://www.freepik.com/premium-vector/bat-ball_9033832.htm

You must place an X on the pavement/concrete and grass/carpet with chalk so that the ball rests in the same place every time you use the bat. Also place a ‘Y’ so that you hit the ball from the same distance every time.

[Table with columns: Surface, How did I use the bat?, What happened to the ball? with rows for Pavement/Road and Grass/Carpet]

Assign students different roles in performing this investigation. Be caring towards those with physical and other learning challenges so they are included and given a role.

[The students will realize that the harder you hit the ball, the further it may go and depending on where you hit the ball, it may go across the ground or go up in the sky. The rougher the surface, the harder the ball moves.]
[The activity can be used and modified with any sport that has a bat]

How did force affect the movement of our ball? When you hit the ball with the bat, how did it move? How did the way you hit the ball affect its movement (The ball moved faster, it also changed direction)
Forces cause objects to change speed and direction. The harder you hit the ball, the more force applied, the faster it moves.

There were some other forces working on the ball too. When the bat hits the ball, it squashes inward a little bit as it is made from light material. The force from hitting the ball causes it to change shape slightly. Because the ball is made from a stretchy material, it rapidly expands again, returning to its original shape and it flies into the air. This is also a force. When the ball hits the ground, it squashes slightly again then returns to its original shape. Forces can cause changes in shape!
Other Types of Forces That Change Shapes

When a force acts on an object, the object may change shape by bending, stretching, or compressing - or a combination of all three shape changes. Squeezing your juice or water bottle changes its shape, pulling on a rubber band or even when you flatten a piece of playdough.
You are also exerting muscular force when you use your arms to move the bat to hit the ball. Muscular force is the force exerted by the muscles of the body.
When you lift a book, you are exerting muscular force.
When you walk you exert muscular force onto the ground.

[Muscular force is the force used by the muscles, which involves legs as well as arms. It is the type of force that occurs due to the body muscles’ action and makes them contact the surfaces. Some examples of muscular force are pulling a toy truck, playing tug of war, jumping, or weightlifting. Bouncing a fall is another example of muscular force.]
It is the type of force, which is exerted when something is touched, and then force is applied to it. In other words, muscular force is related to the movement within the body.

What other forces acted on our balls? Let’s find out! When the ball is at rest on the road or pavement, its weight is a force pushing downward, there is also the force of the pavement pushing upwards. Gravity is also acting on the ball keeping it on the ground. Gravity is a force that is always pulling objects down. The upward and downward forces are the same, so the ball does not move. There is balanced force as the same amount of force is acting in opposite directions, that is, no net force. Do you think gravity acts on the ball when you hit it and it moves upwards into the air? (Yes, because it came back down to the ground).

Let’s learn more about gravity from a video: As you watch this video, think about answers to these questions: a. Which scientist discovered Gravity? (Sir Isaac Newton) b. Where was he and what happened to him that caused him to have an Aha! Moment? (Sitting under a tree when an apple fell and hit him on his head) c. Does gravity affect only light objects? (No heavy and light objects all fall to the ground due to gravity). No matter where you hit or throw a ball gravity gets it back to the ground. Gravity is a force that can affect an object from a distance (without touching it).

Frictional Forces
How is the speed of the ball affected by the change from pavement/road to grass/carpet? (Slows down) Why do you think the ball slowed down? (Grass/carpet is rougher)

Does the type of surface affect the speed or motion of an object?

As the ball rolls across the surface, it scrapes against the grass or concrete and gradually stops. This is because of the force of friction. Friction is the force which opposes motion of one object over another. It is a contact force. Friction steals the energy of the ball because the ball is in contact with the pavement/grass bringing it to rest.

(Materials: meter rule, chalk, carpet, grass, timer, recording sheets/chat)

(This activity can be used to integrate Mathematics standards on time and measurement)

Let us pull a chair across the classroom 3 meters. Now let us pull the same chair across the carpet/grass 3 meters as well. We will time how long it takes us to pull the chair from one point to the next on each surface? (Time can be measurement using a timer or counting out loud)

Alternatively – put thirty seconds on the timer and have the same student move the chair across the tiles/pavement and then carpet/ grass, then measure how much distance was covered using a string and a ruler or a meter stick).

First, you will write down your prediction. Next, we take careful measurements. Each team/group will take each measurement twice. We record our measurements in the table/chart. (Bar charts can be created to display the data – Mathematics link)

[Table with columns: Surface (with images of Pavement/Road and Grass/Carpet), How far do I think the chair will move? (Prediction), How far did the chair move? (Measurement) with Trial 1 and Trial 2 columns]

[Table with columns: Surface (with images of Pavement/Road and Grass/Carpet), How long will it take the chair to move the distance? (Prediction), How long did it take? (Measurement) with Trial 1 and Trial 2 columns]

[Bar chart showing Time/Length vs Surface]

Let us discuss our results!

Which surface took the longest for the chair to move from one point to another? (Grass/carpet)
Why? (The amount of friction generated depends on the surface. A rougher surface will cause more friction than something smooth.)
Extend the discussion on friction.

Dry friction (sometimes called surface resistance) occurs when two solid objects touch each other. If they are motionless, it's called static friction. If there is motion, it's known as kinetic or sliding friction.
Put your hands together so the two palms touch each other. This is an example of dry/surface friction.
Is friction always good? How do we solve problems of friction?
Rub your hands together. Let’s keep doing it for about a minute.
How do your palms feel now? Touch your neighbour’s palms, how do they feel? (Hot)

When friction occurs, it causes an object to slow down and lose its energy. But energy doesn't just disappear, instead, it changes. The moving energy (or kinetic energy) changes into heat energy. This is why your palms were warm!

[In lots of different sports, we don't want friction. This is because it slows us down. Like a cyclist in a race, the friction we know as air resistance between the cyclist and the air can affect their speed. But there are ways to reduce this, like crouching low on the bike and wearing special streamlined clothing. More about what that means down below.]
Let us complete the following worksheet and talk about some surfaces that increase or reduce friction. grade-3-gravity-friction-c.pdf
Circle one: • You will slide faster if there is more / less friction on a playground slide.
• Which material would make more friction if you rubbed your hand on it? tile / carpet
• To slow down a speeding bike, brakes create a little / a lot of friction against the tires.
• If you tried to walk on ice you might slip because there is too much / too little friction.
• It is easier to pedal a bike on a dirt trail / a smooth road because there is more / less friction.
Friction is also unhelpful on a bike if the chain has not been oiled because this creates friction between the moving parts of the bike. This makes pedaling more difficult.

The reason why putting oil on the chain of the bike reduces friction is because it creates a much smoother surface between the chain and the other moving parts, and that means less friction.] https://www.twinkl.co.uk/teaching-wiki/friction
Let us learn more about friction: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IkmAnLmXENo (3:58 minutes)

[In machines and engines, friction is usually quite unhelpful. The constant rubbing of one part against the other wears out even very hard and durable metals like stainless steel. That's why engineers use lubrication: by squirting liquids and gels between moving parts, we can help them slide past one another with less friction, reducing wear-and-tear, cutting maintenance costs, and reducing energy losses.] https://www.explainthatstuff.com/motion.html (For the advanced student)
Magnetic Forces

Do you see the notes that have been placed on the fridge? Have your parents ever placed notes on the fridge door for the family to see? (Yes/no) What other things are placed on the fridge door at home? (pictures, report card etc.)
What do you use to stick a note on the fridge door? (magnet)

Why does the note or picture you stick on the fridge not fall? (A Magnet is attracted to the fridge door, there is a force exerted between the magnet and the fridge that is able to hold up the note or picture).
Let us view a video that explains what magnets are and how they work.
Video link: https://youtu.be/7HHs98PBgk0?si=k3jgBO1bhiMeICHv (5:44 mins) Stop at 1:05 seconds.

What are magnets? (Magnets are pieces of metal or rock with an invisible force to attract special kinds of metal.) This force is called magnetism. A magnetic field is the area around a magnet that has magnetic force. All magnets have a magnetic field, no matter how big they are. Things that are attracted to magnets are called magnetic objects.
Let us see if we can find items around the school that are attracted to magnets. We are going to go on a scavenger hunt.
Retrieved from: https://www.nypl.org/events/programs/2023/09/09/scavenger-hunt

[Give out a magnet to each of your students/ group of students and have them wander around the school (both inside and outside) to see how many magnetic objects they can locate. Let them write down all the objects that they found that attracted their magnet. You’ll be surprised about how many things are magnetic: doors, door frames, metal poles, doorknobs, fences…].

After the students return to their seats in the classroom, discuss the scavenger hunt and what they found. Ask each group to tell the class the various items they found that were attracted to the magnets by asking: What objects were attracted to your magnet? Use a table on the chalkboard/whiteboard to create a class list of items that were attracted to the magnet and those that were not
What type of items were attracted to the magnets? What material are they made from? (metals) How did you know the object was attracted to the metal? (felt the pull of the magnet to the object)
Which items were not attracted to the metal? What materials were they made from? (Plastic, glass, wood)
Do all metals attract magnets?
Provide students with samples of aluminium, silver, copper, iron filings, steel, nickel and allow them to test these pieces of metal with a magnet.
Were all the metals attracted to the magnets? (No)
Which metals were not attracted to the magnet? (Aluminium, silver, copper)
Which metals were attracted to the magnet? (iron, steel, nickel)
All magnetic objects are made of metal, but not all metals are magnetic.
Provide the students with two magnets to play with. They will notice that some ends of the two magnets attract each other and the other two repel.

Retrieved from: https://www.britannica.com/science/magnetic-pole
How many ends does a magnet have? (A magnet has two ends.)
One end is called the north pole and the other end is called the south pole.)
What happens when the north and south poles of a magnet are close to each other? (attract)
There is a magnetic field around each pole.
A magnet is a material that has a magnetic field. A magnetic field is an invisible force that draws objects to a magnet. Magnets pull things made of steel or iron toward them. A magnet has two ends. One end is called the north pole and the other end is called the south pole. There is a magnetic field around each pole.
Let us use our magnet on the iron filings to see our magnetic field. We can also observe how the magnetic force moves our iron filings. Instructions found on this video:

Retrieved from: https://youtu.be/j8XNHIV6Qxg?si=emobNOyqTa6ZQG5y
Is magnetic force acting on the iron filings balanced or unbalanced force? Why? (Unbalanced, iron filings moved around)
Let us make our own magnetic slime and then test to see if it attracts other objects.

Magnetic slime recipe
https://www.survivingateacherssalary.com/how-to-make-magnetic-slime-recipe-stem-activity/

Introduction: Students, I want you to think about a swing on the playground or in a local park or someone’s backyard. Imagine you give one big push to the friend. Describe in your own words what you expect to happen. (the person will move forward until they lose energy and then return, almost to the starting point). Draw a line that shows the path of the person on the swing. Its not a straight line is it? We call that line an arc which is something we will talk more about in mathematics. We would call that arc path their motion and it resulted from you applying a force. Forces are pushes and pulls. For the swing we both push and pull on the swing to make our friend go higher! What other forces cause things to move? What about the force of gravity; it pulls objects to the earth’s surface. We have seen that magnets can also pull some metal objects along. Did you know we can put magnets in a motor and make it turn when we apply electricity? That is an amazing invention that relies on the pull of the magnet force.

Back to our swing example...we apply a force and we start the motion of the swing. How did you know the person will return to the starting point? (I have used swings many times and they always go out so far and then return) What does their path depend on? (the length of the rope holding the swing). The path of the swing “forward and back” is predictable from our experience; we would say there is a definite pattern to that motion. You probably know the distance you walk to the store in your neighborhood. It is very likely that it takes almost the same amount of walking time each visit you make to the store. Because you probably walk the same speed most times, we would say there is a pattern and we can predict how long it will take you to get there.
Today we are going to look at patterns in motion all around us everyday. You have already shown me that you are clever students. You can predict patterns!
Let us review then: What is motion?

Definition: Motion is the process of an object moving or being moved. Motion that repeats is said to occur in a pattern. We can observe patterns of motion to make predictions about how things will move in the future. The force that slows down moving objects is called friction.

What is a pattern? Definition: A pattern is something that happens or appears in a regular and repeated way. Patterns exist in various forms. They can be found in mathematics, the arts, science, language, and social science. A pattern can appear as a visible design, such as a spiral or set of stripes. Patterns can also appear as repeated events, such as in the weather or in the behaviors of living things.

Describe patterns in the environment (natural and human). Patterns help children make predictions because they begin to understand what comes next. They also help children learn how to make logical connections and use reasoning skills. Patterns can be found everywhere in our daily lives and should be pointed out to small children. The sun came up and went down and then the moon came up and went down. The frog hopped up and down - sunrise and sunset.
The water cycle – rain falls down to earth as precipitation and goes back up in the sky to form clouds - evaporation and condensation.
The way a caterpillar moves.
A jackhammer.
A seesaw.

Predict what should come next in patterns. In math, a pattern is defined as a sequence of repeating objects, shapes or numbers. We can relate a pattern to any type of event or object. A pattern has a rule that tells us which objects belong to the pattern and which objects do not belong to the pattern. Let’s look at some examples of patterns: Retrieved from: https://www.splashlearn.com/math-vocabulary/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/pattern-image-1.png

Create an AB pattern: A pattern is a sequence. A repeated arrangement of numbers, letters, shapes, colors etc. AB patterns are patterns that involve two varieties of objects or colors or numbers. AB pattern worksheets provide a sense of order in understanding and identifying recurring patterns and help develop critical thinking in kids.
Types of Motion: The types of motion are straight line, zigzag, up and down and back and forth.
Types of motion diagram: Retrieved from: http://pt.slideshare.net/safa-medaney/what-is-motion-and-its-types

Straight Line Motion: Moving things follow different paths. Some things move in a straight line. Other things move in circles. Straight line motion is a change in position from one point to another in a straight line (in one dimension). A pattern of motion is the repeated process of an object moving. Patterns of motion can be used to make predictions. Today we will engage in activities and discuss one way that objects move.
Activity One: In this activity you will choose a ball. You will roll the ball at varying distances. Now you will describe the motion or how the ball moved.

Example of guided questions for class discussion: 1. Did the ball move fast or slow? 2. In what pattern did the ball move? (The ball moves in a straight line). 3. Did the pattern change at any time? (No the ball always rolled in a straight line).

You will now roll the ball to different targets, for example a wall, a desk at varying distances (short, medium, long). How did you get the ball to roll to the various targets? (More force or a harder push makes the ball roll faster and further in a straight line).
Now we will engage in a competition. The person who throws or rolls the ball the furthest (distance) will be declared the winner.

Activity Two: Let us look at some pictures that show some objects in our everyday lives that travel in a straight line. (Teacher will then provide various pictures of objects that move in a straight line for discussion). Teacher will question students about the activity in each picture. Example:
How will the arrow travel when the girl lets it go? (When she lets go, the arrow goes in a straight line and then when it loses energy, it begins to make an arc until it falls to the ground).
Retrieved from: https://www.acaedu.net/cms/lib3/TX01001550/Centri city/Domain/441/Stemscopeediamovement.pdf
Will the bowling ball continue travelling in a straight line?
A rolling ball is one of the prime examples of linear motion in everyday life. When a muscular force is applied to a bowling ball, it rolls along the lane in a straight line.
Retrieved from: https://studiousguy.com/linear-motion-examples/
Do fruits fall in a zig zag or straight line from a tree?
A coconut falling from a tree will be an example of straight-line motion because it will fall vertically downwards in the gravity following a straight line.
Retrieved from: https://nikobens.wordpress.com/2014/05/14/coco nut-tree/
Zig Zag Motion: Another way objects can move is in a zigzag motion.
Activity One: Use your pencil to draw a line and connect the dots on your worksheet. Follow the number sequence. What
Retrieved from: pinking-shears-for-sewing-tailoring-and-quilting-blades-can-be-re-positioned-167100572.html
Retrieved from: https://www.istockphoto.com/vector/dog-chasing-its-tail-gm182026993-27569592
Retrieved from: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zigzag#/media/File:NO_road_sign_102.1.svg
Activity Three: We will now play a game called ‘Stay on the Path.’
Retrieved from: https://www.twinkl.com/resource/my-community-maps-resource-za-ss-79

Instructions for game: You have in front of you a map of Dan’s Neighbourhood. Today Dan decides to do something different and take a zig zag path to get to the park. If Dan starts at his house, we can trace the route that Dan would have to take in order to get to the park? Use your pencil to map out Dan’s journey. (Make a left onto Bishop Lane, then right onto Cooper street, left onto Lark Avenue then right onto Rose Street.)
Circular Motion: Circular motion is described as a movement of an object while rotating along a circular path.
We will now be looking at another way objects move. Students will now look at objects that move in a circular motion.
Activity One: Show students a variety of pictures and then name them together. a ceiling fan, a standing fan, a car wheel, wheel on the fridge
ferris wheel
merry go round
After observing the pictures, ask students: Have you ever seen or used one of these items? (yes/no)
Are they in motion in the pictures? (no)
If they were in motion, how would the pattern move? (in a circle/circular)
Have them brainstorm other things that move in a circular motion. (fan on planes, propellers on boats, planets moving around the sun, an analog clock)
Option: students can be taken on a field trip or nature walk as they search for things around them that move in a circular motion.

Activity Two: We will conduct a simple experiment in this lesson. I would like you to collect these materials from the table at the front of the classroom. (They will be given one uncut plate and a marble as well as a triangle cut plate and marble.)
Gently push the marble along the rim of the plate and carefully observe the force making the marble go in a circle is the normal force of the plate rim on the marble.
Questions: What was the force that caused the marble to move? (a push)
After pushing the marble on the plate, what motion did it move? (circular-then spiral)
What allowed the marble to move in a circular motion? (the rim of the plate)
They will repeat the experiment with the triangular cut plate. When the marble reaches that section it no longer travels in a circle but goes off in a straight line.
Before beginning the experiment, What is different with this plate compared to the first? (we cut a triangle from the plate)
Do you think the marble will move in a circular motion like the first plate? (no)
Explain why? (this is because the plate is missing a piece that will be needed to guide it around)
What do you think will happen to the marble when we push it? (it will not go in a circle)
After: What happened to the marble when it got to the cut out? (It left the plate in a straight line)
What if we eliminate the force, what would happen? (The marble would not move unless it is unbalanced)
Side to Side Motion We can say that motion is the change of position of an object with respect to time. A book falling off a table,

water flowing from the tap, rattling windows, etc., all exhibit motion. Our daily activities, like walking, running, closing the door, etc. involve motion. There is a change of position of the object involved in these activities. Even the air that we breathe exhibits motion!

Forces cause all motions. Every time the motion of an object changes, it’s because a force has been applied to it. Force can cause a stationary object to start moving or a moving object to change its speed or direction or both.
Today we will be observing the motion of some everyday objects. Are there patterns in the way things move? Let’s find out using marbles!
Activity One
Experimenting with marbles
Materials: large, deep glass bowl and a marble (Can be done in groups or as a demonstration)
In this experiment we are going to try to predict the motion of a marble. When we predict, we are sharing what we think is going to happen.
If we were to place the marble in the center of the bowl, what do you think would happen? Write down your prediction.
If you were to place the marble on the left side of the bowl near the top, what do you think would happen? Write down your prediction.
Let us try this out.
[Student will drop the marble into the bowl. Studnets will observe the marble as it moves slightly further and further away from the center, and up the sides. Students observe its motion and record.]
Retrieved from: https://profmattstrassler.com/articles-and-posts/particle-physics-basics/tunneling-a-quantum-process/
What happened to the marble that was placed in the bottom of the bowl at the center? (Moves slightly and eventually is at rest)
What happened when the marble was placed on the side of the bowl? (It swings from side to side and eventually it stops)
Did it continue swinging from the same distance every time? (Swinging movement got shorter and shorter)
When you placed the marble closer higher up the side of the bowl, did it affect the motion of the marble? (Yes)
How? (Moved faster from swinging from side to side/ same pattern of motion but faster)
We can see that the motion pattern is repetitive.
If I were to place the marble closer to the edge of the bowl, can you predict its pattern of motion? A pattern of motion is the repeated process of an object moving.
Let’s observe! [Students observe the marble being dropped from a greater height, very close to the edge of the bowl.] (Same side-to-side movement but at a faster speed.)
Why is the marble moving faster? (Greater height, larger force is exerted increasing the speed)
Why is the speed of the marble reduced and the height of the swing gets shorter as time goes by? (Friction, the motion of the marble against the wall of the bowl slows it down, reducing its energy)
Activity Two
Pendulum
Making a simple pendulum with everyday materials: https://youtu.be/aPOmduWJlAg?si=OoRhdiyoaAarumwo (1:25 mins)
***Can also be done in Arts and Craft.
How to make a pendulum
We have created a pendulum. Play with it for a minute. Observe its motion!
How would you describe the motion of the pendulum? (Swings back and forth/ side to side)
Is this motion repeated? (yes). The pendulum moves in a pattern.

Have you ever seen a pendulum before? (Yes/No) Have you seen any object at home or at play that has a pendulum as part of it? (Student share: pendulum clock, amusement park rides, wrecking ball, metronome)
Let’s investigate how a pendulum works!
How can you make the ball of our pendulum move faster? Write down your suggestions in your activity sheet.
Let us test our ideas using our pendulum. We will test our ideas in an orderly way by writing down the position that we drop our ball and what happens when we do so.
When a pendulum is set in motion, gravity causes a restoring force that will accelerate it toward the center point, resulting in a back and forth swinging motion. The path of the pendulum is an arc.
How will changing the length of the rope affect the movement of the ball?
What happens if I use a heavier ball instead?
The teacher will encourage students to bring a pair of magnets to school and engage. They will complete each as demonstrated on the worksheet and on the line they will record their answer

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Ask questions to determine cause and effect relationships of electric or magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other. [Clarification Statement: Examples of an electric force could include the force on hair from an electrically charged balloon and the electrical forces between a charged rod and pieces of paper; examples of a magnetic force could include the force between two permanent magnets, the force between an electromagnet and steel paper clips, and the force exerted by one magnet versus the force exerted by two magnets. Examples of cause-and-effect relationships could include how the distance between objects affects the strength of the force and how the orientation of magnets affects the direction of the magnetic force.] [Assessment Boundary: Assessment is limited to forces produced by objects that can be manipulated by students, and electrical interactions are limited to static electricity.]

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: Magnet, Invisible force, Lines of force, poles, static electricity, magnetic interactions, electrical forces, attract, repel, permanent magnets, temporary magnets, electromagnets
Identify objects attracted by a magnet.
Understand that magnets have two poles.

Identify different types of magnets and understand that magnets have two poles, north and south. (When two like poles (N-N or S-S) come in contact, they will repel. When two unlike poles (N-S) come in contact, they will attract.)
Demonstrate the effect of static electricity on objects.
Compare the force exerted by one magnet compared to the force exerted by two magnets.
Explain the use of magnets in the home and the community
Distinguish between magnetic and non-magnetic materials.
Match simple gadgets (magnetic) to the use to which they are put. (ST 2 TE TM 2)
Observe and demonstrate the effects of magnets on the motion of different materials (e.g. iron filings, non-metallic materials). (ST 3 PS FMS 3)
Describe and demonstrate the forces push and pull. (ST 3 PS FMS 1)
Identify, with examples, forces at work in everyday situations in the home and community. (ST 3 PS FMS 2)
Match with relevant examples, properties of materials which make them useful for certain specific purposes. (ST 3 PS MM 4)
Investigate the ways in which different forces can change the speed and direction of a moving object. (e.g. magnetism, static electricity) (ST 4 PS FMS 1)
Account for how the concept of static electricity can explain simple balloon experiments.
Differentiate true north and magnetic north as it relates to the use of a compass for navigation.
Observe how magnets attract or repel each other and attract some materials and not others
Compare and group together a variety of everyday materials on the basis of whether they are attracted to a magnet
Investigate how the orientation, size and distance of magnets affects the strength and the direction of the magnetic force.
Appreciation for magnets in everyday life
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.
Identify and appreciate that the gadgets, tools and structures used in their homes and community are made by humans. (ST 3 TE UT 1)

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Pre-instruction assessment: All magnets have the ability to attract other magnets or magnetic objects (such as iron, nickel and cobalt and their alloys). Try this activity to learn more about magnetic force.
Materials needed: paper clip, eraser, scissors, two or more different magnets
1. Tie a length of thread to a metal paper clip so that thread hanging is 10 cm long.
2. Place a magnet on the table
3. Hold the paper clip approximately 5 cm above the magnet

4. Gradually and gently lower the paper clip. 'What did you observe? What did you feel? What did you see?' (As the paperclip is lowered, the paper clip is attracted to the magnet and moves towards the magnet)
5. Repeat with the eraser and then the scissors
https://www.liveworksheets.com/w/en/science/953382

Exploring Magnets worksheet: Complete the sentences using the word bank below. Magnets have two __________. One is called the __________ pole and the other is the __________ pole. When opposite poles are near one another, they __________ together. This means the two poles __________. When two of the same poles are near one another, they __________ away from one another. This means the two poles __________ each other. Word bank: repel, north, attract, south, pull, push, poles. Draw arrows to show what force happens in these pictures.

Below is a worksheet. Each student or group will receive two sheets. One will be used to make predictions as to whether the items are magnetic or non-magnetic. For the second worksheet, students will be given a magnet to test the items and indicate whether their predictions were accurate. This will be a class discussion.

Check off the box to show which are magnetic and non-magnetic. (Worksheet with objects: Wooden blocks, Metal Paper clips, Buttons, Pom poms, Nuts and bolts, Ruler, Staples, Eraser, Wrench, Rocks, E-C coin, Keys, wooden Sticks, Dice)
Answer to worksheet
Complete the sentences using the word bank below. (Magnets have two poles...)
Draw arrows to show what force happens in these pictures.
Magnets in the World: Have students do research and find at least ten examples of how magnets are used to solve problems in the real world. They should come to class prepared to report.
The Earth is a Magnet with Invisible Lines of Force: The earth has a North and South Pole similar to a magnet. It also has invisible magnetic field lines much like a bar magnet.

The earth’s magnetic field allows us to use a compass for navigating because the compass needle always points to the north. Have students do research to find out what the term “true north “ means with regard to the earth’s magnetic field.
Static Electricity in the World: Have students do research and find at least five examples of where you would see static electricity in the real world. They should come to class prepared to report.

Mary has a problem: Mary likes to wear dresses to her workplace. One day she walked into her carpeted office and sat down in her office chair, only to find her skirt sticking to the back of her legs. How irritating! Describe why and how you think Mary might have a problem with static electricity. (she collects charge by walking across her carpet and her legs must be neutral like the wall; therefore they are attracted)
Distinguishing Invisible Forces: Let us now complete a short activity. Read each short scenario and tick whether its caused by static electricity or a magnet.

Extended Activities for Student Research: Did You Know? 1) That a photocopier uses a charged drum to attract the powdered toner ink for making copies! 2) That a very large magnet is used for getting images of our bodies when we aren’t feeling well? (magnetic resonance imaging.) 3) That magnets are used to make high speed trains by levitating the train above the rail (repulsion) thereby reducing friction? See: Maglev trains at: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maglev
inclusive_learning_strategies: Students, I want to show you an amazing invisible force. Look at the special apparatus I have made.
Retrieved from personal pictures (G. MacKinnon)
Why do you think the paperclip is suspended in the air like this? (I think there is a magnet in the top that is attracting the paperclip.)

If I take a piece of paper and add iron filings to it (iron is what nails are made of) and place a bar magnet underneath it will produce a special pattern where the magnet is attracting the iron filings.
Retrieved from: https://www.exploratorium.edu/snacks/magnetic-lines-of-force
The patterns you see are called lines of force.

Let us go back to our initial demonstration. Watch as I use a pair of scissors and cut the lines of force. The paperclip falls! Did I really cut those lines? (no teacher, the scissors got in the way of the invisible force)

What else could we put between a magnet and the paperclip. Let us try a piece of paper, a ruler, a cloth, cookie sheet. (things that are metal seem to break the force lines but nonmetal objects don't.)
Today we are going to investigate invisible forces even more.
Review: What are magnets and how do they work?
Materials: 2 closed boxes, box A and Box B - one with iron nails and the other with toothpicks, bits of paper and glass marbles).
The two boxes will be passed around the classroom so students can shake the two boxes and feel the mass of each box.
Alternative: provide boxes to students in small groups.
Question: What type of materials do you think these objects in box A and box B are made from? Why do you think so? (Write down student’s suggestions)
Possible responses: box A has metal objects. The objects make a clang/ringing sound. Box B has nonmetal/wooden/plastic objects. They make a much softer sound.
Students will then be shown two identical magnets.
What are these objects called? (Magnets)
What are magnets? How do they work? Students share what they remember about magnets.
Let’s watch a video that explains how magnets work. https://youtu.be/7HHs98PBgk0?si=0wdGSqSn5HCvuGpg (up until 2:06 of 5:04 mins)
Give students 2 magnets to observe and test.
What did you notice about the magnets when you try putting them together? (They come together on one side but push away when I try the other side)
Why do you think they do different things on each side? (They have two sides, They are not the same)

A magnet has two sides that are called poles. A north pole and a south pole. No matter how hard you try to force two of the same poles together, they will push away, for example: north pole to north pole. We say
that they repel when they push away. However, if you flip one of the magnets around so that opposite poles now face each other, the two magnets will attract each other and come right together.

Magnetic and Nonmagnetic Materials: What do you think will happen when we place the magnets at the bottom of box A and box B? (The objects in box A will be attracted because they are metal objects. The objects in box B will not be attracted to the magnet.) The teacher will allow the students to observe what happens when a magnet is placed under box A and box B. Why were the objects in box A attracted to the magnet? (Metal objects). These objects are made from magnetic materials. Why were the objects in box B not attracted to the magnet? (Nonmetal objects) Let us now look to see what objects are inside our two boxes. What materials are these objects made from/ (Wood, plastic, glass) These are nonmagnetic materials. Magnetic materials are those which are attracted to a magnet e.g. some metals such as iron. Non-magnetic materials are those which are not attracted by magnets e.g. wood, paper, plastic.

What would happen if we mix our objects in box B and Box A? How would we be able to separate the magnetic materials from the non-magnetic materials? Allow students to mix the objects and then attempt to separate them. (students may hover magnet over the box; students may move the magnet below the box; students may place the magnet into the box) How did you separate the objects? (Only the iron nails were attracted to the magnet, so we were able to remove them) Materials can either be magnetic or non-magnetic

Magnetic and non-magnetic materials: Materials that are attracted towards a magnet. Materials that are not attracted towards a magnet. What types of objects attract magnets? (Metals) Do all metals attract magnets? Provide students with samples of aluminium, silver, copper, iron filings, steel, nickel and allow them to test these pieces of metal with a magnet. The students will use the following table to record their observations. Objects that can be used: iron nails, aluminium foil, sterling silver jewelry, galvanized nails with zinc coating, copper wire, 25 cents piece - has over 60% copper, steel wool.
Were all the metals attracted to the magnets? (No)
Which metals were not attracted to the magnet? (Aluminium, silver, copper) Which metals were attracted to the magnet? (iron, steel, nickel)
All magnetic objects are made of metal, but not all metals are magnetic.
Let’s now classify objects as magnetic and non magnetic.
Types of Magnets
Materials needed: paper clips/staples, permanent magnet, nail, dry cell, wire.
Guiding question: How can you make a nail into a magnet?
The students will be given some paper clips (5) or staples, a magnet and a nail.
Use the paper clips/ to demonstrate when your nail has become magnetised.
Allow students to explore and be creative with the objects provided.
How did you make your nail magnetised? (stroke the nail in one direction and lift the magnetic off the nail after each stroke)
The students will demonstrate to the class when their nail is magnetised.
Students view this video on How to Magnetise a Nail https://www.youtube.com/watch?app=desktop&v=ok9GkzRiymM (1:27 mins)
Guiding question: How can we increase the strength of our magnetised nail?
Let us see which group can pick up the most paper clips/staples. Count how many times you rub the nail to the magnet.
How many strokes did it take before the nail picked up one, then several paper clips/staples?
You created a temporary magnet. This allowed you to use the nail to lift the paper clips/staples.
What is a temporary magnet?

Temporary magnets are objects that have magnetic properties for a limited time period under certain conditions. They are only able to create their own weak magnetic field while in the presence of a stronger magnet. Temporary magnets are objects made from iron and iron alloys (mixture of metals). However, when the field is removed, the object gradually loses its magnetism
Retrieved from: https://www.thesciencehive.co.uk/magnetism-gcse
We have just learnt about temporary magnets. Magnetics can also be permanent.
What are permanent magnets?
Permanent magnets are made from metals like iron or nickel that are naturally magnetised and have their own magnetic fields. Once they are magnetised, they do not easily lose their magnetism.
Some of the magnets you have been using are permanent magnets.
Can you identify the permanent magnets?
Have these permanent magnets lost their strength over the time we have been using them? (No).
This is because they are permanent magnets.
Let us watch a video on the types of magnets. How many types of magnets are there? Can you identify any other type of magnets?
Students will watch the second part of the video https://youtu.be/7HHs98PBgk0?si=s3vTHaoLEVdefAsv (2:05 - 2:28 mins)
Did you learn about another type of magnet? what is it called? (Electromagnets)
There are three types of magnets: temporary magnets, permanent magnets and electromagnets.
What are electromagnets? (see: https://kids.kiddle.co/Electromagnet)

An electromagnet can be created when electricity flows through a metal. They are magnets that are only magnetic when there is a coil of wire that has electricity flowing through it. The magnetic force starts when electricity flows and stops when the electricity is no longer flowing.
Let us see if we can make our own electromagnet.
Students will be given a wire, battery and a nail to create their electromagnet. They will test their electromagnets to pick up paper clips using their electromagnet.
Retrieved from: https://www.shalom-education.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/image-74.png
How can we make our electromagnet stronger?
Students will be given more batteries, longer wires and bigger nails, galvanized nails and iron nails to try to create a stronger electromagnet.

Electromagnets can be made stronger by adding more coils to the copper wire, or adding an iron core through the coils (for example a nail). The current can also be increased to make the magnetism stronger. (Taken from https://kids.kiddle.co/Electromagnet)
Computers, TVs, and electric motors use electromagnets as well as burglar alarms, electric relays and fire bells.
Use of Magnets
Magnets are used very much in the world arounds us.
Students, can you identify the uses of magnets?
Teacher can access uses here: https://youtu.be/7HHs98PBgk0?si=s3vTHaoLEVdefAsv (2:28 - 4:01 mins)
Forces of Magnets
Today we are going to observe and compare the force exerted by one magnet compared to the force exerted by two magnets. We are going to engage in a simple experiment.
Magnet Experiment: Are Two Magnets Twice as Strong as One? In this experiment we will determine whether the strength of the magnetic field created by two magnets is twice

the strength of the magnetic field created by one magnet. For this experiment you will gather the following materials to work in your groups.
- 2 identical magnets
- metal paper clips
- Stack of index cards, sticky notes, or paper
- Stack of felt or cloth squares
- Ruler

Instructions
1. Take one of the magnets and place an index card in between the magnet and the paper clip. Does the magnetic force of the magnet hold up the paper clip through the card? Record the data and your observations in a chart like the one below.

2. Add another card between the magnet and the paper clip and record your observations.
3. Keep adding cards one at a time until the magnet cannot hold the paper clip.
4. Count how many cards are between the magnet and paperclip.
5. Repeat the experiment using squares of felt or cloth.
6. Repeat both parts of the experiment using 2 magnets stuck together. When you combine magnets, you will need to use a magnet that does not have any casing so that they will stick together.
7. Graph your results. Try making a bar graph with “Number of Magnets” on the x-axis and “Number of Index Cards” or other material on the y-axis. This will help you determine if two magnets are twice as strong.

Discussion questions based on the results.
Does the material between the magnet and the paperclip matter? Why? Is the magnetic force greater when there is more than one magnet?

Conclusion of the experiment: two magnets together are stronger than one magnet, but two magnets will not be twice as strong as one.

Experiment retrieved from: https://www.education.com/science-fair/article/two-magnets-twice-strong/#:~:text=Two%20magnets%20together%20will%20be,add%20the%20magnetic%20fields%20together

Magnet Experiments: The Effect of Orientation, Size and Distance

Experiment 1

I want you to think about a fridge magnet. What happens when you bring it close the refrigerator door?

(It begins to pull towards the door) When is the pull the greatest, when you are close or far away? (When we are close) We would say that the magnetic force gets greater when the distance is smaller.

The amount of magnetic force depends on distance.

Closer magnets are, the stronger the force.

On a magnet, the force is the strongest at both poles (North and South)

As objects move closer together, the magnetic forces between them increases.

Like poles repel, opposite poles attract.

Experiment 2
For this experiment you will need: Magnets, iron filings and pieces of paper.
Instructions
Put two magnets underneath a piece of paper with 1 inch apart.
1. Carefully and slowly shake some iron filings onto the piece of paper above the magnets.
2. If the iron filings are too far from the magnets, gently tap or shake the paper to move them closer to above the magnets.
3. Observe how the filings move and distribute on the paper.

4. Next, remove the iron filings or carefully pour them back into the bottle.
5. Flip one of the magnets upside down.
6. Repeat steps 1-4 to see what iron filing pattern is formed this time.
Retrieved from https://www.rookieparenting.com/magnetic-field-science-experiment/
Another Invisible Force: Static Electricity
Did you ever walk across a carpet and then watch as a spark jumped from your finger to a nearby friend or piece of furniture? That spark was electric charge moving from the carpet through you to another object!
That type of charge is different from electricity in the wall where electric charge is moving all the time. We call this spark that you experience static electricity.

Let’s now find out what it is.
Static electricity is the buildup of the electrical charge in an object when it is rubbed against another object.
Teacher background: Electric charge gets transferred from objects that are electron donating to those that are electron receiving. To judge which objects are likely to donate electric charge, one must refer to the triboelectric series.
Retrieved from: https://www.sciencelearn.org.nz/images/3879-triboelectric-series
From this chart you can see that rabbit fur would readily give electrons to a plastic rod. Or human hair would donate to a rubber balloon. (a popular, readily available
combination is to rub a rolled-up plastic transparency in hair or fur
Static electricity causes objects to stick together when they have opposite charges and repel when they have the same charge.

Demonstration 1: Based on our table above, if we rub a balloon in our hair, it picks up electric charge. If we begin to pull the balloon away from our head, it pulls some hair towards it because the hair has given away the charge and the balloon has received it. They are opposite in charge, so they attract- our hair seems to pull away!
Demonstration 2: If we charge up two balloons separately by rubbing them in our hair, they will have the same charge and the balloons will repel each other!

Demonstration 3: A wall is overall neutral in charge. It does not have an excess of electric charge. If we rub a balloon in our hair, the balloon will have electric charge and be attracted to the wall because of the positivity in the wall.

Static Electricity Simulation: If the teacher has access to a computer, they may do a demonstration or set up a learning center for their students using a computer simulation of static electricity. See: https://phet.colorado.edu/en/simulations/balloons-and-static-electricity/about

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Activity 1: Answer the following questions: 1. Which animal is a member of a a. Herd (cow, elephant) b. Flock (bird, sheep) c. Swarm (bee) d. Pride (lion) e. Troop (monkey) 2. What are TWO reasons why some animals form groups? (for protection and food or to survive) 3. “Animals can survive better when they are alone.” Discuss the above statement by stating ONE thing that you agree or disagree with.
Worksheet on Why Animals Form Groups Retrieved from: http://www.varsitytutors.com/3rd_grade_science-help/life-science/why-animals-form-groups

Question #1: Why Animals Form Groups Possible Answers: They enjoy having friends, They do not form groups, To help them survive, To do as they are told. Correct answer: To help them survive. Explanation: Animals form groups to increase their chance of survival. The animals work together to hunt, raise their young, protect each other, and more. Survival would be much more difficult if some animals lived on their own rather than in a group.

Question #2: Why Animals Form Groups Meerkats live in large families. Some are look-outs while others care for the young. Other meerkats are in charge of digging tunnels, and some are hunters. The meerkats will trade jobs and take turns.
Why does living in this large family benefit the meerkats?

Possible Answers: The meerkats don't have to do as much work, so they get to be lazier. The meerkats like having company around and other animals to play with. There are more members to split up the work and help with survival needs. There is no benefit to meerkats living in family groups; they just do it out of habit.
Correct answer: There are more members to split up the work and help with survival needs.

Explanation: Living in a large family benefits the meerkats because they can split up the work and have members of the family working together to keep the family safe and healthy. Rather than all of the work falling on a few animals, it can be split up amongst dozens. Living in large groups like this help with protection, food collection, and general survival.
Question #3 : Why Animals Form Groups Elephants live in large herds. When traveling, they keep the babies in the middle of the line or center of the group.
How do these large groups help babies survive?
Possible Answers: These groups do not help babies survive. The group allows babies to drink more water. These groups protect babies from predators. The group keeps the babies from seeing the scenery.
Correct answer: These groups protect babies from predators.

Explanation: Large herds of elephants can be seen moving across the plains of Africa with their babies in the middle of the group's lines as they travel. If predators attack them, the babies are usually targets because they are smaller and weaker. If the female elephants keep them hidden and guarded, they are protected. This technique is to help babies survive.

Question #4: Why Animals Form Groups Fish are one of many animals who form groups for survival. They will form "bait balls" with hundreds (even thousands) of fish to scare away predators and make it more difficult for predators to catch them.
Fish form these groups to help them ____________.
Possible Answers: swim, hunt, splash, survive

Correct answer: survive. Explanation: Fish form these "bait balls" to distract their predators and make it more difficult for them to be eaten. This technique is all about survival. Forming groups makes each fish safer than if they were swimming alone.

Question #5: Why Animals Form Groups. How do groups help animals to survive? Possible Answers: It does not help them to survive; it makes their lives worse. It helps them to find food, care for young, and protect one another. It helps them to feel less lonely, have more fun, and play together. It allows them to look cooler like they have more friends. Correct answer: It helps them to find food, care for young, and protect one another. Explanation: Living in groups provides animals with many benefits. A large group of animals is less likely to be bothered by predators, there are more females to care for and protect the young, and more members to look for food sources.
Working together provides them with more opportunities for survival than living alone does.

Question #6: Why Animals Form Groups. Which answer choice(s) is a benefit of animals forming groups? Possible Answers: All of the answer choices are correct. Animals can assist each other with jobs and tasks when in a group. Animals help each other raise the babies in the group. Animals can help to protect each other when in groups. Correct answer: All of the answer choices are correct. Explanation: All of the answer choices list a benefit to animals forming groups. When animals form groups for survival, they can help each other raise the young, hunt, keep watch, gather food, find shelter, and survive. Animals in groups have more eyes looking for predators or prey. They also have less work to do individually because they can share the workload. Animals in groups often survive better than if they lived alone.

Question #7: Why Animals Form Groups. Monkeys live in family groups that can grow quite large in numbers. If monkeys lived alone, there are some tasks they could not complete. Based on the photo below, what is a task that requires a group to help with? Possible Answers: Eating, None of the answer choices require a group, Grooming, Sleeping. Correct answer: Grooming. Explanation: Monkeys are very social and live in large groups with extended family. Monkeys help each other groom and use it as a chance to spend time with each other. Without a group to help the monkeys could not groom themselves properly. They rely on each other for help with their survival needs.

Possible Answers: A single fish would stand out and be easier to catch than a fish mixed in with thousands of others. Fish prefer to be in groups because it prevents them from being lonely and sad. There is no reason for a fish to join a "bait ball" instead of swimming alone. A fish can swim faster and longer when it is in a group compared to being on their own. Correct answer: A single fish would stand out and be easier to catch than a fish mixed in with thousands of others.

Possible Answers: They could die without the group to help them. They could become very mean. They could do all of the different jobs by themselves. They could get lonely and find new friends. Correct answer: They could die without the group to help them.

Rather than all of the work falling on a few animals, it can be split up amongst dozens. Living in large groups like this help with protection, food collection, and general survival. If the meerkats were forced to live alone rather than in family groups, they might die. If they are busy gathering food or digging tunnels, they may not see predators. It would be hard for a meerkat to do all of the jobs by itself.
