Ecosystems: From observing the different habitats, you would have seen that each habitat has different characteristics. We can group the things found in a habitat as living things and non-living things. The living things (plants and animals) found in a habitat are known as the biotic components. On the other hand, the non-living elements like rocks, soil, air, water, etc. in the habitat are referred as the abiotic components of a habitat. Living things in a habitat, not only depend upon each other for their survival, they also depend on the non-living things found in their habitats. We say that the organisms and the other components of their habitat, make up a system in which the parts depend on each other. This is called interdependence. However, in order to survive, the living things must interact with the non-living things. These interactions along with the physical environment within which these organisms are found is referred to as an ecosystem. Within an ecosystem, one or more habitats can be found. Let us now look at a tropical rainforest ecosystem in which many different habitats can be found.

Habitats Rainforest: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fGXYULMDpSk (1:30 min). A tropical rainforest has many different types of plants and trees of varying heights. Starting from the ground up these plants and trees make several vertical layers in which organisms make their habitat. In a rainforest you may also find soft earth, rivers and a variety of animals such as frogs, snakes, birds, monkeys, sloths, jaguars and more. The rainforest has many different habitats.
Photo showing a Forest: Photo retrieved from https://pxhere.com/en/photo/1542143
Here is a story about Timber the Monkey and the Ecosystem in which he lives. https://youtu.be/qr_U1P7XwhI?si=sY19z_zgauvXHqAg (1:05 mins)

Timber the Monkey: Photo retrieved from: https://www.pexels.com/photo/brown-monkey-sitting-on-tree-branch-3560352/. Timber the monkey lives deep in the forest with his family. He spends most of his time high in the trees eating juicy fruits and playing with the other members in his troop. He and his family love to help each other remove lice and other insects from their fur, those times are the best.
Question: How is Timber’s body adapted for life in the forest? Watch the video below to observe a monkey: Cool Facts About Monkeys https://youtu.be/A6lmmAyvl3I?si=Kuh9iwniF2mZCaIP (2:05 mins)

Timber and his family made the forest their home because they have adaptations which help them to survive there. They all have long limbs that are great for climbing trees; long tails which they use to swing from the branches and a fur colour that allows them to hide between the leaves of the tree.

Question: How do you think the forest habitat meets Timber’s needs? Do you think monkeys, like Timber, help to meet the needs of other organisms? The trees in the forest offer Timber and his family shelter, food and protection. The trees in turn benefit from Timber’s troop when they move from tree to tree spreading seeds which grow to become new trees. As they move they also spread their droppings, which provides nutrients for the trees. Timber feels safe when

he's high in the trees but on occasion he has to leave the tree to drink water from the nearby river. Visiting the river can be dangerous for Timber because of the many predators that also live on the forest floor and in the river.
Question: What other organisms would you expect to find in Timber’s ecosystem?

Timber and his family are not the only animals that live in the trees of the forest. There are also iguanas, snakes, various insects, frogs and birds but of all the creatures he has met, he finds Boo the Sloth to be the most fascinating.
Photo retrieved from: https://www.rawpixel.com/image/3305450/free-photo-image-costa-rica-animals-sloth
Question: What features make it possible for a sloth to survive in the same habitat as a monkey?
Watch the video below to observe a Three Toed Sloth https://youtu.be/OTp8W251aiQ?si=ZWQkGSApS5QKbNoS (1:55 mins)

Like Timber the monkey, Boo has long limbs but his limbs are tipped with giant claws which he uses to climb the trees. His colouration allows him to hide against the tree branches but instead of eating the fruit of trees he prefers to eat the leaves, twigs and buds. He also uses the forest for food, shelter, protection and to get his water. However, unlike the agile monkeys that leap and swing through the trees with ease, Boo the sloth moves extraordinarily slowly. Timber enjoys swinging in circles around Boo. He wants to play with Boo, but Boo is just not interested in his monkey games.
Millie the Jaguar
Photo retrieved from: https://stock.adobe.com/search?k=jaguar+tree

Millie the Jaguar is hungry, what should she eat? She has an excellent sense of smell and vision. Millie is built to be a superb predator and now it's time for lunch. She stalks stealthily around the base of a tree where she just saw a monkey swinging. Who could that monkey be? (Timber) Millie knows she will need to approach the tree very quietly so that the monkey would not get away. She uses her spotted coat to hide between the bushes and dry leaves at the base of the tree. She then leaps onto a tree branch using her sharp claws and strong muscles to climb the tree.
Question: What do you think happens next in the story??
Activity: students work in groups or individually to finish the story by either making a video, drawing, audio recording or short paragraph of the story's ending.
Question: What would happen to the animals in this ecosystem if their habitat is changed in a negative way? (Less or no food, no shelter, no protection from predators)
Can organisms survive in habitats that are not their natural habitats?
From the videos and pictures, we have looked at, you would have observed that the animals that live in the different habitats have special adaptations that allow them to survive in these habitats.

Results from studies done on plants, and animals in their habitats, have caused scientists to conclude that in some habitats, some organisms survive very well, others not as well, while others cannot survive in them at all.
The picture below shows a polar bear in a habitat.
Something is wrong here?
Photos retrieved from: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Polar_Bear_Sitting_%286292639587%29.jpg, https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Wadi_Rum_Dry_Desert,_Jordan.jpg
1. Is this the polar bear’s natural habitat?
2. Will the polar bear be able to survive in this habitat as well as the animals that live there?
3. What features/adaptations of the polar bear make it difficult for it to survive in this habitat?
4. What adaptation would the polar bear have to undergo for it to be able to survive in this habitat?
5. Give reasons for your answer.
6. What about the camel, do you think it will be able to survive in the habitat that the polar bear lives in?
7. What is the name of the habitat which the camel survives well in?
8. What is the name of the habitat that the polar bear survives well in?
9. Compare both habitats and state two ways they are different and two ways they are similar.

While the polar bear may not survive in the desert, or the camel may not survive in the arctic region, some animals or plants may be able to survive in other habitats, even if they may not be able to survive as well as they would, in their natural habitat. Let us look at a couple of examples.

1. Alligators are reptiles that live in freshwater habitats. They need warm, wet environments to live in. They move from water body to water body. They thrive well in swampy areas. They are however able to survive in saltwater for short periods.
Which of the following habitats would the alligator be able to live in but not as well as if it was living in its natural environment?
a. A grassland
b. A shoreline
c. A tropical rainforest
d. A desert
Explain your answer.

2. A cactus thrives very well in the desert. Which of the following habitats will it be able to survive in but not as in the desert? a. A grassland b. The arctic region c. A tropical rainforest d. A desert Explain your answer. Questions for discussion: Discuss which of the following habitats humans survive well in, survive less well in and cannot survive in at all: 1. grassland 2. ocean 3. polar region 4. desert What are some of the things humans do to survive in harsh environments? Exploring an ecosystem close to home By this time in the unit, students would have watched several videos about different habitats, the animals and plants that live in them and how these habitats provide their basic needs. Note to teacher: To help students learn more about adaptations, take them to a nearby ecosystem such as a pond, stream, park, rock pool, field, forest, etc. Ask students to describe the ecosystem. For example, at a stream, students might observe that it is made up of water, rocks, mud, sticks and dead leaves. When students are finished describing the ecosystem, ask them to observe and describe the organisms they observe living in and around the ecosystem. They should be encouraged to take pictures and videos. Students should also be reminded not to remove plants, rocks, animals, from the ecosystem as they explore it. Here are some questions that the students could be required to answer as they explore the ecosystem. 1. List the animals that you observed living in the ecosystem. 2. List the plants that you observed living in the ecosystem. 3. Select three of the plants you have seen living in this habitat, then answer the following questions. a. Are the plants growing inside the water, around the pond or are floating on the water? b. How are these plants adapted to survive there? c. How does the habitat meet the needs of these plants? 4. Select three of the animals that you have seen living in this habitat, then answer the following questions. a. Are the plants living inside the water or around the pond? b. How are these animals adapted to survive there? c. How does the habitat meet the needs of these animals (What do the animals eat? How do the animals get air? Where do they hide from predators, etc. Habitat Game See simulation of habitats here: https://ssec.si.edu/habitats

inclusive_learning_strategies: What is Environmental Change? In our last set of lessons, we looked at different habitats, the organisms that live in them and how these habitats meet the needs of these organisms. Each habitat had a different set of features and a different set of organisms living in them. The conditions and features of the habitat in which an organism lives is called the environment. The environment can however undergo changes or disturbances, we call these changes environmental changes. Let us now watch a video to learn more about environmental changes: https://youtu.be/94cA_JLDcFk?si=Rly-Tv2xvcOFjTtq (7:03 min). As you watch look out for the following: What is an environment? (Certain conditions in which plants, animals and humans live). What are some examples of environmental change? (weather conditions, landform changes, human development of land and waterways, access to food and shelter). What can cause environmental change? (humans, animals and [natural events]). From the video, you would have seen that environmental changes can occur slowly or very rapidly. You would also have observed that these changes can be caused by nature.
(natural events) the actions of humans and animals.

Teacher Note: If you don’t have access to technology, pose the following questions about these two captures from the video. What happens to living things when the desert gets too much rain or the rainforest gets too little rain? (this poses a change to the environment which will affect plants and animals) A closer look at environmental changes caused by natural events

Note to teacher: If your country has undergone changes and there are students in the class who may have experienced these changes or seen the result of these changes, this could be used to initiate a discussion on environmental events and their effects on the environment. The discussion can then be expanded to include other natural events.

Teacher: Students you would have studied some natural events in the previous grade. Some of these were shown in the video, can you list them? Do you know of other natural events? List students’ responses on the board. Videos or pictures can be used to help students describe what happens with each natural event. For example, a volcanic eruption releases ash, lava and hot gases, while a hurricane brings with it a lot of rain and wind and could cause flooding. After students have gone through each natural event, they could then discuss possible effects that each natural event could have on the environment and on organisms.
Teacher: Let us look at some of the environmental changes that each natural event could have on different habitats and the organisms living in them.

Activity I will now assign one of the natural events and a habitat to each group. Working in your group, discuss the effects that you think the natural event you have been assigned will have on the habitat you and the organisms living in it. At the end of your discussion, you will make a five-minute presentation to the class.

Environmental changes caused by the actions of humans While natural events can cause a significant amount of damage to the environment, humans also cause damage to the environment in many different ways. As a matter of fact, humans impact the environment more than any other species. Can you think of the ways that humans change the environment? Watch the following video to introduce the concept: https://youtu.be/mhtB7nJKugU?si=kBM20rT00GGB-C5S (1:16 min) deforestation, clearing of lands, draining of swamps, release of industrial waste, fires, introduction of certain species, overfishing, overhunting, water
pollution, improper disposal of garbage, air pollution, introduction of diseases, mining and the use of pesticides, insecticides, and fertilizers.

Teacher Note: If you don't have access to technology, show the students the following pictures (from the video) and ask them to identify how this human change may affect living things in the environment.
Let us look again at the first video which shows some of the impacts of the actions of humans on the environment. See if you can identify them.
https://youtu.be/94cA_JLDcFk?si=Rly-Tv2xvcOFjTtq (Timestamp- 3:10 min to end)

Some of the actions of man that could cause negative changes to the environment These include: Loss of habitat due to cutting down of trees, mining, clearing of land for agriculture, roads, etc. Habitats become fragmented (broken up into smaller units) by building roads, installing power lines, or even changing the course of rivers or other waterways. This is known as habitat fragmentation.
Contamination of water bodies due to dumping of garbage, release of factory waste, fertilisers, volcanic ash, etc.
Air pollution due to gases, dust, soil particles, etc. released into the air.
Pollution is the introduction of harmful materials (pollutants) into the environment.
Soil erosion
Destruction of coastal areas, coral reefs, etc.
Drying up of streams and rivers
Loss of some species due to overfishing, overhunting, migration, etc
Introduction of invasive species (new and harmful species)

As you can see from the situations above some environmental changes may affect the environment in a negative way which may lead to further changes to animals, plants and other features of the environment. For example, animals or plants may die or lose their homes and the nonliving structures of the environment may be destroyed or damaged.
Environmental changes may include changes to the land characteristics, water distribution, temperature, light, food and the number and diversity of the organisms living in these environments.
How organisms are responding to changes in the environment
Animals can react to change in their habitats in only three ways: They can move, adapt or die.
How animals are responding to changes to ecosystems
Some animals are moving to higher ground with cooler temperatures to avoid the heat.
Some animals, however, seem to be adapting to changing conditions.
Some migratory animals are delaying their migration time
As spring arrives earlier, some insects are emerging earlier.
Some migrating birds are laying their eggs earlier to match insect availability so their young will have food.
Some species such as corals are adapting to the warmer temperatures.
Some animals are waking from hibernation sooner.
How plants are responding to changes to ecosystems

Unlike animals, plants cannot move. When the conditions around them change, they cannot head elsewhere to find better conditions; plants have to adapt on the spot to survive or die. These are some of the ways that plants are adapting to environmental changes.
Increasing temperatures and drought have brought about earlier bloom times.
Growing in areas that they normally do not grow.
Having a shorter life span.
Growing towards light or water.
Activity: Choose two of the different ways that animals are adapting to changes in the environment and state how these are helping the animal or plant to survive in the changed environment.
New species are moving into the damaged area
While some animals are moving to new locations or some are dying, in some cases,

new species are moving into the damaged environments. These species are better adapted to living under these conditions. They are sometimes referred to as pioneer species. How is this a good thing? How would these new species help the species that are there?
How are changes in the environment leading to animals and plants becoming endangered or extinct

If animals or plants cannot adapt to any of these changes in the environment, extinction (the loss of an entire group of organisms) of that type of animal or plant can occur. Once a species is extinct, it can never be brought back.

Over the years, several animals and plants have gone extinct. This is mainly due to habitat loss, diseases, overhunting, overfishing, overharvesting, pollution, etc. some species have become endangered or extinct.
What are endangered species?

Endangered species are living organisms whose population sizes have declined to critical levels, putting them at risk of extinction. These species are categorised as endangered when their populations have decreased to such a degree that they are likely to disappear entirely from their natural habitats if appropriate conservation measures are not implemented.

Examples of some organisms that have become extinct include: 
- The Caribbean Monk Seal
- Japanese Sea Lion
- Baiji
- Rivero's barn owl

Activity: Procedures
1. Hide the beads around the room while the students are outside.
2. When they return to the classroom, ask them to hunt for the beads on three different occasions.
3. After each hunt, ask students to count how many beads they found.
4. Discuss the number of beads found each time they looked for beads. They should realise that each time the beads were harder to find and there were fewer of them. The same is true with some of the animals on the earth. There are fewer and they are more difficult to find – that is why they are called endangered animals.
5. Make a list of endangered animals. Why are they in danger?
6. Go to the library and find more information on endangered animals in your country or in the OECS region.

Here are a few examples of organisms in the OECS that are endangered
The Grenada Dove
The St. Vincent Whistling Frog
The Big Leaf Mahogany
The White Breasted Thrasher (St. Lucia)

Endangered Species Puppet Show: Let the children make puppets of their favourite endangered animals. Put on a puppet show. Ask the children why their animals are endangered and what people can do to save them.

There is hope: From the videos and pictures you have seen that there is environmental destruction (i.e. damage to the environment) everywhere and that more and more environments are being destroyed and several species are either endangered or extinct. However, all is not lost. Many organizations and individuals in your country and around the world (including students like you) are working very hard to protect the environment, reduce habitat destruction and the impact it can have on organisms. So, what are some of the ways that this is being done?

Conservation and preservation: Conservation describes efforts by individuals, communities, corporations, governments, and other groups to conserve the natural environment. With environmental conservation we are trying to use natural resources in the best possible way. Preservation includes protecting the natural environment from being disturbed in the first place. It is the protection of natural areas from human activities. Conservation and preservation efforts include: Protecting habitats especially nesting and denning areas, and wildlife habitats. Setting up man-made homes for different animals. Limiting and modifying agricultural activities. Encouraging individuals to care for any wildlife in their area. The passing of laws and legislation. Monitoring of species/habitats.
Encouraging individuals to get involved in conservation efforts, for example turtle watching, beach clean-ups, tree planting, etc.
Restoring habitats to increase local biodiversity and species population.
Development of environmental educational resources to increase awareness.
Finding and rescuing animals on the brink of extinction.

Note to teacher: To help students understand the conservation and preservation efforts taking place in their country, the region and the world over, personnel from the Ministry or department responsible for the environment, or persons from environmental groups can be invited to the classroom to talk with student on these issues. Following the session, students can be asked to find out about the following and to submit a report in whatever format they please. Examples should not include those given by the presenter. 1. Two examples of disturbed areas in your country.
2. Examples of protected areas.
3. Two laws in your country developed to protect habitats from negative environmental changes.
4. Two things that are being done to protect species and habitats in the world.

A closer look at Ecosystem Restoration
Can we repair some of the damage humans have done to habitats and the organisms living in them? Yes, ecosystems can recover from destruction. Ecosystem restoration (i.e. ecosystem repair) seeks to do just that by repairing disturbed ecosystems through human intervention.
Ecosystem restoration accelerates the recovery of an ecosystem that has been disturbed.
Watch the following video on Ecosystem Restoration to learn more. As you watch look for some of the things that can be done to restore ecosystems:
https://youtu.be/JYNpHEVCVb4?si=Z116eHE53Ev4Gg7F (1:32 min)
Can you think of any ecosystem restoration projects that may be happening in your community? Or are there any projects that you think need to be done to restore any part of a damaged ecosystem in your community?

Let us now look at an example of a restoration project carried out by students
To help us to understand how a simple restoration project can be carried out, we will now look at a project that was undertaken by some primary school students just like you.
Note to teacher: Consider having a group of students roleplay how the project was conducted.
Students attending a primary school located near to a park which had a stream running through it, noticed that the park was not being used by the residents and that there was a lot of garbage dumped in the stream. It was a very nice park and the students and their teachers felt that something should be done to restore it to its former state and to stop the dumping of garbage in the area.

Picture showing the garbage dumped in the park’s stream
Photo retrieved from:
https://www.americanrivers.org/2022/10/teaming-up-to-take-care-of-urban-streams/
After conducting a biodiversity study of the plants and animals found in the area, the students analyzed their data to determine what can be done to restore the stream to its former state.
They then wrote letters to several environmental groups in the area and shared their findings. Following this, they carried out a survey in the local community, produced a brochure which they distributed to the residents.
With the help of their teacher, the students used mathematics to work out what it would cost to purchase the resources they needed to restore the area.

They then prepared presentations so that they could speak to the different environmental groups and to the residents. This helped them to attract some funding, which was used to restore the area. Some of the residents also volunteered to help. After the area was restored, the students continued to monitor the area.

Suggested questions for discussion: 1. What are some of the effects that the garbage dumped in the stream could have on the plants and animals living there? 2. Why do you think it was important for the students to involve the residents of the areas in their plans? 3. Why do you think it was important for the students to share their findings with environmental groups in the area? 4. What are some of the organisms that could be found in the stream and other areas of the park? 5. What are some of the reasons why a person may be dumping garbage in the stream?

6. What are some of the things that the students and members of the environmental groups can do to ensure that the restored park does not return to the state it was in? 7. When students go to the areas to monitor it, what are some of the things they would be looking for to see if their solution is working? 8. What is some of the information they should include in their brochure?

Developing a plan to help to restore an area that has experienced an environmental change Activity: Divide the class into groups of three to four students, give each group pictures or a video on their tablet of an area that has experienced an environmental change. Working in their groups, students will then develop a plan on how to make positive changes to the area. They should consider the following: - The people who may use the area and think about how they might use it. - The other living things that may use the area when planning. Draw a plan of how you would change the area for the better.
Each group would then be asked to present their proposed plan of action.
Members of environmental groups in the area can be invited to listen to the presentations and to give feedback to the students.

Assessment of a site that has undergone an environmental change on which a solution for the restoration of the said site has been implemented The goal of habitat restoration is to try to return a particular habitat or ecosystem to conditions as close as possible to what it was before it experienced an environmental disturbance. Before the restoration project can be carried out, one has to determine what damage has been done and what needs to be done to restore the area. After this, one has to draft a plan of action for a possible solution for the restoration which must include how the success of the solution is to be determined.
Note to teacher: For this activity, students can be taken on a field trip to a site which has sustained an
environmental change and to which a solution has been implemented, to help to reduce the impact of the change.

If this is not possible a report (preferable a video report) on a similar site can be looked at and discussed. Pictures of the site before and after the solution was implemented could also be compared and discussed. Additional information can be had from various ministries and organizations that may be involved in the implementation of the solution.

Before students begin the assessment, information on the characteristics of the habitat/ecosystem and the plants and animals which lived in the habitat/ecosystem before the given environmental change occurred, should be provided to students where possible.

From information obtained from direct observation, through videos, research, books, pictures, etc., students could be asked to do the following: 1. Describe the characteristics of the environment at the moment. 2. Identify the changes in the environment. 3. Describe what problem the change in the given environment caused for the plants and animals that were living there, and solutions introduced to deal with the problem. 4. Identify and describe the effect of the solution on the plants and animals within the environment. 5. Identify and describe the resulting changes to plants and animals living within that changed environment, after the solution has been implemented. 6. How well did the solution help to reduce the impact of the problem created by the environmental change in the system? 7. How did the changes the solution made to one part of the environment, affect the other parts of the system? 8. What is your conclusion on the solution to the problem? Do you think that it was effective? Give reasons to support your answer.

An Amphibian is a cold-blooded vertebrate animal of a class that comprises the frogs, toads, newts, and salamanders. They are distinguished by having an aquatic gill-breathing larval stage followed (typically) by a terrestrial lung-breathing adult stage.
For amphibians in their life cycle, they are often in contact with water. Because amphibians also breathe through their skin, they are particularly sensitive to chemicals in the water.
It has also been found that fertilizer run-off into waterways cause certain parasites to thrive and these infect frogs causing malformations in their life cycle.

Butterfly Puppet Play: To activate student’s sense of wonder around the life cycle of butterflies, create simple puppets and perform the following puppet show. Scarlet The Butterfly: Scene 1: Scarlet the little caterpillar is talking with her puppet friends about the weather changing and her ferocious appetite. She is merrily indulging in a delicious meal of leaves on a well-endowed branch. She draws attention to her brand-new pink bow. Scene 2: Scarlet the large caterpillar, is found by her friends moving very slowly along a leafless branch. She is complaining of a full tummy and the ensuing cold weather. Her friends suggest they enjoy the fall because of the many apples to eat. Scene 3: The fall weather is upon us. Friends of Scarlet, (bundled in warm clothing) come by looking for Scarlet, calling her by name, searching her out, paying no attention to the pupa hanging on the branch above. Of course, there is no response from Scarlet. At this point little viewers (your students) are telling the puppet friends that the pupa must contain Scarlet, because they see her pink bow hanging from the pupa. In disbelief the friends disperse still calling out for Scarlet.

Scene 4: On the same leafless branch, Scarlet appears before her friends as a butterfly. They are surprised that she knows their names but having talked to her and seen the pink bow, they realise that it really is her! They ask her where she has been all winter and she explains that she could hear them calling from inside her cozy pupa. She explains how she makes the pupa to ready herself for the long winter. She invites them to come and watch next fall when she is sure other caterpillars will be preparing for the pupa stage of their life cycle. The puppet friends are amazed at Scarlet’s transformation and the forms she assumes as she progresses from a little caterpillar to a butterfly. Have a discussion with students that identifies the stages of development of the butterfly. Emphasize that the life cycle process of a butterfly is called metamorphosis.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Students, have you ever heard the words life cycle? What do you suppose it means? If we break down the two words, it suggests a cycle which means something that repeats or goes round and life. What does it mean to have life? We are alive, what are the characteristics of us as living things? We breathe. We eat and grow. We move. When we grow to be adults we sometimes have children. We react to touch, sound, light & smell. We are affected by our environment. These are the characteristics of living things!
Look at this picture of a life cycle of a human male.
Retrieved from: https://www.freepik.com

Why is this considered a cycle? (we start as a small human when we are born and eventually when we reach old age we will die and if we have children it starts all over again). We say that by having children we are propagating or producing more humans. Another word for that is reproducing humans in a cycle of life.
Animals that have lifecycles can be classified as vertebrate (with a backbone or spine) and invertebrate (without a spine) Some examples of each are shown below.
Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?app=desktop&v=KjpGfqqvQ3E (3:36 mins)
There are five main classifications of vertebrates namely: fish, amphibians, reptiles, mammals and birds.

Unusual Vertebrate Cycle: Here is an interesting fact about one vertebrate’s life cycle. At first, a baby kangaroo grows inside its mother. But when it is no bigger than your little finger, the baby is born. The tiny baby crawls through the mother’s fur into her pouch where it grows into a joey (a young kangaroo). Kangaroo Life cycle video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UpsnREY-6no (2:23 min) Ask students why the small kangaroo has to live in the pouch? (not fully developed as yet-needs milk to grow) Pictures retrieved from: https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=1277722

A Range of Life Cycles: Students let us look at these examples of life cycles and see what we find that they have in common. Teacher Note: Some of these pictures are taken from several books which you may find useful to have in your classroom. Watch it Grow: Backyard Life Cycles by Barbara Reid Read Aloud here: https://us02web.zoom.us/j/83648714002 (7:08 mins) Amazing Life Cycle of Butterflies by Kay Barnham Amazing Life Cycle of Plants by Kay Barnham Life cycle of a butterfly is called metamorphosis.

Observations: eggs start small & increasingly grow, their looks change, which is a cycle that produces more of the living thing (reproduction). More on Propagation (continuing the living thing): We have seen before that plants can propagate or reproduce by creating seeds during their life cycle. These then drop into soil and with the proper conditions (soil, moisture/heat) can grow again as another plant.
The goal of every living organism, including plants, is to create offspring for the next generation. Animals and the wind can physically move seeds from one place to another and promote propagation.
Flowers can propagate by insects assisting in a process called pollinating.

Pollination is the act of transferring pollen grains from the male anther of a flower to the female stigma. Example: Bees in seeking out sugar (nectar) in flowers accidentally can brush against the flower’s parts and allow other partner flowers to create offspring seeds. Teacher Note: see more details of pollination below in additional materials.

Angiosperms are plants that have flowers and seeds encased in fruit. Angiosperms can be divided into two major categories, monocots and dicots. The first leaf to emerge from the seed is called a cotyledon and it can be single leaf (monocot) or double leaf (dicot) (see more detail in additional resources below).
Students, let us look at pictures that show life cycles of plants. Watch it Grow: Backyard Life Cycles by Barbara Reid Read Aloud here: https://us02web.zoom.us/j/83648714002 (7:08 mins)

Natural Vegetative Propagation Class, did you know that plants can also grow and reproduce without seeds? They do this through a process called vegetative propagation. This is when plants reproduce from stems, roots and leaves. There are natural ways that plants make more plants!
Retrieved rom: https://www.sciencelearn.org.nz/resources/1662-vegetative-plant-propagation
Bulbs Bulbs, such as daffodils, form lateral buds from the base of the mother bulb, which produce new smaller bulbs or bulblets in subsequent years.

Rhizomes Rhizomes are root-like stems that grow horizontally under the ground. New roots and shoots form at the nodes with shoots growing upwards to form new plantlets. Lateral buds grow out to form new rhizomes. Examples include iris and root ginger.

Stolons Stolons or runners are horizontal stems that grow above the ground, for example, strawberries. Tiny plantlets form along the stolon, and roots form where they touch the ground. When the connection with the parent plant breaks, the new plant becomes independent.

Tubers Tubers are swollen portions of an underground stem that store food so a plant can lie dormant over the winter, for example, potatoes. Axillary buds, commonly known as 'eyes', form over the surface of the tuber and produce shoots that grow into a new plant the following year.
Leaf Propagation Plants like Bryophyllum (leaf of life/ miracle leaf plant), have buds coming out from the notches of the leaves. These buds develop into new plants.

Artificial vegetative propagation Gardeners also use vegetative propagation methods that plants don't use naturally. These methods involve taking a piece of one parent plant and causing it to regenerate itself into a new plant.

Cuttings A cutting is a piece that has been cut off a mother plant and then caused to grow into a whole plant. Often this involves a piece of stem that is treated with hormones to encourage new roots to form before planting. Sometimes root cuttings with buds on them will produce new shoots when pressed directly into soil.

Grafting and budding This method involves joining a stem piece (as in grafting) or a single bud (as in budding) onto the stem of a plant that has roots. The stem piece or bud is called the scion, and the plant with roots is called the rootstock. Grafting is commonly used to produce fruit trees sometimes with more than one variety of the same fruit species growing from the same stem.

Layering In the layering method, one of the branches of a plant is pulled down into the ground and covered with moist soil in such a way that the tip of the branch remains above the surface. After a few days, new roots start growing from the part buried in the soil. This part of the branch is then separated from the parent plant to let it develop into a new plant.

Extended activity: World Problems: A Debate on Solutions. Scientists now have the capability to create rice and corn that have traits of drought and disease resistance. These experiments involve changing the building blocks of these foods at the smallest level, something called the genes. Genetically modified organisms (GMOs) can be defined as organisms (e.g., plants or animals) in which the genes have been altered in a way that does not occur naturally through normal parenting. The Cons: Some people in the public believe that manipulating these genes is very dangerous. They are primarily worried about the long-term effects of these changes. Will the improved corn/rice cause us problems in our diet? Will it affect animals that eat GMOs also? The Pros: Some people believe that creating these new crops will help the developing world and especially those faced with starvation due to poor crop yields when there is lack of water or a rise in plant diseases.
Students can write a one-page response paper after researching opinions on the internet and speaking with local elders about indigenous perspectives.

Why does an elephant have big ears? (To help keep it cool in hot sunshine) 4. Why does a shark have a white belly. Adaptations That Assist Birds: A Beak Experiment. Birds have a range of beak types, all for different purposes that fit their habitat and living condition. To understand the importance of matching a beak to the life survival of a bird, students will experiment with different simulated bird “beaks” to determine which beak is best for each food item. Students will use a toothpick, net, tweezers, spoon, scissors, and clothes pin to pick up various objects. The objects can be gummy worms, seeds, string, uncooked macaroni, grass, etc. Students will determine which beak works best for each food and why.

Adaptations Project. Students will be asked to do research on at least three living things (plants or animals) in their country that have clear adaptations to the Caribbean habitat. Students have a choice of a poster or PowerPoint ® to include the following:

Plants That Adapt for Protection: Plants may have barbs, spines, thorns, irritating sap, poisonous fruit etc. to protect themselves against being destroyed by animals. Have students identify examples in their community of plants that have these properties, adapted for protection.

Plants That Adapt as Predators: Certain plants have adapted parts so they may actually attract, trap and digest insects. Have students do research on the Venus Fly Trap. Video resource here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O7eQKSf0LmY (2:50 mins)

Reproduction and Local Bird Species: Many birds will have bright colors and exhibit dramatic behaviors in order to attract female counterparts to create offspring. Have students make a poster of all the colorful birds in their region. The teacher can designate a bird to each group to research for pictures and typical habitat. As an extended activity, students may search the internet for videos (to share in class) of courting behaviours of Caribbean birds.

Adaptive Behaviors in Animals: Around the world, animals are approaching humans more confidently. The improper disposal of garbage has many animals leaving their forest habits and venturing close to dumps and family homes. When surprised or alarmed these animals can be dangerous. As a class, the students could design an initiative to keep animals safely away from homes by managing garbage disposal in their communities. Example: Retrieved from: https://youtu.be/iJoRl26eYrc
inclusive_learning_strategies: Students did you know that science has been very helpful in improving agriculture and food production around the world? Here is just one example. The green tomatoes in the picture are able to resist diseases that would normally kill the plant. The red tomato is another variety which grows very quickly.

Scientists have been able to combine the characteristics or traits of parent plants to create offspring plants in a process called hybridization. The tomatoes below are the offspring of the green and red tomatoes above. But the offspring plants are now able to prevent disease and grow quickly. These are useful traits. All tomato pictures captured from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GwjNhYGREN4 (1:58 mins) We know that plants and animals are constantly involved in reproduction so mixing of traits from parents can happen frequently both naturally (mixing of colored flowers in the wild) and artificially (using science experiments) This is a very important technique that scientists have developed. Around the world the best traits of plant species have combined in experiments on such food items like rice and corn. Plant Review In order to understand traits that plants might have, it is useful to review what we know about plants. There are different classifications of plants. The are flowering plants and non-flowering plants. Flowering plants include all the flowers around us including those plants that bear fruit. Some examples are trees, shrubs, herbs and climbers. Non-flowering plants use other ways to reproduce and they include mosses, ferns and cone bearing trees (coniferous trees). Teacher note: For overview see: https://www.youtube.com/watch?app=desktop&v=n5eoI9Pcoyg (3:38 mins) and https://www.youtube.com/watch?app=desktop&v=FbrjnHm-CnI (4:13 mins)

Plants have parts with distinct functions: They have roots, whose function is to bring nutrients and water from the soil but also to hold the plant firmly in the ground. They have stems that transport the water and minerals from the roots up to the leaves. They have leaves that gather the energy from the sunshine and make food for the plant (photosynthesis) As plants develop, they can have different shapes of leaves such as elliptical, oval and linear. (see additional descriptions below). The leaf shape would be considered a trait of that plant. Animals Have Traits Too! Animals are living things so they breathe, move, respond to stimuli, reproduce and grow. They are also dependent on their environment. Some animals eat meat (carnivores) and some eat plants (herbivores). Students, let us make a list of some of the animals in our country. Choose a favorite animal. What are their traits or characteristics? (size, eye color, body shape, hair color, hair length, facial- characteristics like shape nose, ears etc.) Where do we see Traits Differing in Animals? Read the story (http://hca.gilead.org.il/ugly_duc.html) of the Ugly Duckling or watch the video read aloud here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TyrmcD8Yml0 (9:43mins)

Engage a discussion with the students based on the following questions: Why did the other ducks not like the Ugly Duckling? (He did not look like them, he had different traits and they considered him ugly because he was different) Should we dislike someone if he/she doesn’t look like us? Why? (No, we all can have different traits.) Why did the Ugly duckling seem so odd? (Their traits were quite different from their parents) Why was the Ugly Duckling not able to fit into the group with the other siblings? (His feathers beak and feet were different from the others and he was bigger as well.) Why was it able to fit into the group of swans? (As he grew his traits looked more like swans, white feathers, orange beak and feet, long neck, etc.) Was the egg from which he came, laid by the mother duck? By some mistake, a swan’s egg ended up in the duck mother’s nest. He was actually a baby swan.
Have students observe the pictures of the Ugly Duckling and the other ducks and compare the traits. Then have students observe pictures with the Ugly Duckling and the swans and compare the traits.
Can You See the Traits in the Offspring? Retrieved from: https://nationalcanineresearchcouncil.com/visual-breed-identification/ The father of these dogs may have been black in color?

Field Trip: Take students on a field trip to an animal farm and/or vegetable farm. Let students observe the groups of animals and vegetable plants on the farm. Then compare the traits of the animals and/or plants. Teacher note: Highlight the similarities and differences among the offspring, parent and siblings. Focus discussions on similar and different traits between offspring and parent and sibling and that differences exist between organisms of the same group.

Breeding for Special Purposes: When farmers want stronger horses, they will select parent horses that are the biggest and most muscular. These will become the parents of baby horses. This is called selective breeding. Other examples where desirable traits are achieved by breeding: Cows that give more milk, Donkeys that can walk longer distances, Dogs that are less aggressive, Sheep that produce more wool, Animals that can survive on less water and in hotter climates

What are Your Traits? Even though you may have brothers or sisters from the same parents and bear some similarities in your traits it is possible, quite naturally, that you will have some different traits that have been passed down from your parents. Have students bring in a picture of a parent(s) and have peers identify similarities and differences. Game: mix up the parental pictures and see if the class can separate out who is the parent of each child based on similar traits. Extended Activity: Inventory of traits - see assessment column
The world is divided into zones that have different temperatures during the year.
Zone map Retrieved from: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1e/Latitude_zones.png

Students, look at these pairs of pictures. They are similar species of animals in different parts of the world where the temperature is different (climate zones). These pictures show that temperature can certainly affect the traits of animals; in this case color.
Highland Cow on the left is better suited for a cooler climate with such a heavy fur coat. Retrieved from: https://kilts-n-stuff.com/highland-coo/
The bear on the left lives in a temperate zone and the one on the right lives in polar zone. Retrieved from: https://www.quora.com/Where-do-brown-bears-black-bears-and-polar-bears-live

The colors of winter and summer coats of these deer change because of temperature variation in the same climate. Retrieved from: https://www.wildlifeonline.me.uk/animals/article/water-deer-fur-moulting

Students, do you ever wonder how penguins who live in the south pole are able to dive and swim in very cold water? It is because their bodies have adapted to their environment. Today we are going to investigate not just penguins, but also a host of animals and plants that have made amazing adaptations in order to survive.
Animals live in a variety of home locations we call habitats. Many have adapted to better survive in the conditions that exist there.
Have students watch these videos to learn more about adaptation. Teacher provides questions before the video is watched.
Teacher note: If you do not have access to technology, you may use the captured pictures to engage a discussion of adaptations.
Video 1 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LB8nLZmxN_M (adaptation) 6:19 mins. Questions for students to consider as they watch: What adaptations has the camel made to survive in its desert habitat?
What adaptations has the monkey made to better survive in its forest habitat?
How have fish adapted to aquatic (under water) habitats?
How have penguins adapted to polar (at the south pole) habitats?

Video 2: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fRX2JtKFUzk (adaptation) 2:20 mins. Question for students to consider as they watch: What adaptations has the giraffe made to better survive in its savannah (grassland scattered with trees) habitat?
Long neck for eating off of tall trees.
Long, tough tongue to protect against thorns
Spotted coat to hide it in the trees. When an animal has an outer body covering that hides it from possible predators, we say that they have camouflage.
Many animals have adapted in their appearance by blending into the background. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YOIRci0CKzg (camouflage) 4:00 mins
Students look at these examples from the video: [Images of tiger, walking leaf, shark, leafy sea dragon]

Adaptation of Blubber Keeps Animals Warm: An Experiment to Demonstrate the Effect: Animals in the North and South Poles (e.g. penguins, seals, whales, walrus) have a layer of fat just beneath their skin that helps them keep warm!
Experiment retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_DQGAcqhkGs
We can demonstrate how that fat can affect the heat transfer from our bodies to cooler places. We use sweaters and extra socks to keep our body heat; animals have blubber!
Materials: 2 bowls of ice water, 2 freezer bags, 3 cups of shortening
1. Place the shortening inside one of the freezer bags.
2. Put your hand inside the other empty freezer bag and force your covered hand inside the fat-filled first freezer bag.
3. Move the fat around so that it nicely covers your entire protected hand.
4. At the same time, thrust your fat covered hand in one bowl of ice water while simultaneously putting your bare hand in the other bowl of ice water.

Students that try this, one after the other, will find a huge difference in what they feel on their hands. The fat covered hand will insulate them from the cold ice water the same way blubber insulates penguins from the cold outside temperatures.

Plants also Adapt. The following video gives examples of how plants adapt. Ask students to take note of these as they watch. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mbj_WQ76F1Q (adaptation in plants) 7:02 mins. These captures from the video can be used by the teacher.
Cacti also have: a thick stem to prevent water loss. a waxy coating on the stem to help in the storage of water. A long and deep root system to gather water in lower parts of the soil.
Plants in bodies of water have flexible stems to accommodate movement of water.
Water lilies have air space in their stems so that they are able to float on top of water.
Plants in rainforests often have very large leaves to collect as much sunlight as possible.
Some plants have poisonous flowers or fruit to protect them from animals that may harm them. A good example is the Manchineel tree.
Interesting fact: Manchineel trees are poisonous to birds but not Central and South America's striped iguana, which actually eats and lives among these trees.
Adaptations of Predator and Prey. Some predators adapt to better catch their prey. Have students guess how these predators have adapted?
Scorpion: (sleeps during the day and roams at night nocturnal)
Cheetah: (strong legs, very fast)
Octopus: (changes colour to sneak up on prey)
Prey have adapted to avoid being eaten by would be predators. Students, can you guess how these prey have adapted?
Tortoise: (hides in its shell)
Gazelle: (very fast runners)
Porcupine: (has barbed spines that inflict pain when an animal touches)
Skunk: (sprays a foul chemical on the attacking animal)
Venomous snake: (has poison in its fangs that can kill a predator)

Adaptations that Ensure Offspring. Ask students why they think male birds are often more colorful than their female counterpart? (when male birds compete for a mate so they can have offspring the brightest bird may attract the female. Birds have adapted color and showmanship to gain the female mate’s attention. This adaptation guarantees offspring and therefore survival.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Represent data in tables and graphical displays to describe typical weather conditions expected during a particular season. [Clarification Statement: Examples of data could include average temperature, precipitation, and wind direction.] [Assessment Boundary: Assessment of graphical displays is limited to pictographs and bar graphs. Assessment does not include climate change.]

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners are expected to: Knowledge - Define the terms: Weather, Weather forecasting, meteorologist/meteorology, temperature, precipitation, wind direction, clouds, moisture/humidity, climate, atmosphere.
Demonstrate an understanding of the formation of clouds.
Review and explain the components and processes involved in the water cycle.
Distinguish between weather and climate.
Identify and describe weather patterns at a particular time at a given time.
Identify instruments used to measure the elements of weather.

Demonstrate an understanding of how to use a) thermometer to measure the degree of hotness and coldness, b) anemometer to measure wind speed, c) rain gauge to measure the amount of rainfall, d) windvane to measure wind direction.
Skills - Identify types of weather conditions by examining weather charts. (ST 3 ESS EW 10)
Investigate and report from data collected about the weather over a period, using simple graphs. (ST 4 ESS EW 7)
Conduct a simple experiment to find out if the weather affects evaporation in natural settings. (ST 3 ESS ER 8)
Communicate effectively results of weather experiments.
Analyze a table and bar graph and make predictions based on the average temperature, wind speed and precipitation in a particular area.
To work respectfully with others in exploring and investigating the weather.
Demonstrate a concern for weather conservation as well as protection of our clean water sources.
Describe the weather patterns that affect the activities of people and the effects they can have on our daily lives. Eg flooding, hurricanes, dehydration, sporting activities, drought.
Develop a curiosity of how air, temperature, moisture, precipitation, wind speed and direction make up the weather in a particular place and time.
Based on an understanding of the water cycle, show concern for the protection of water resources, the effects of pollution and climate change.
Work cooperatively with peers.
Participate in classroom discussions.
When conducting practical and group work., display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Review of the Water cycle - Have students label the water cycle diagram below as a review.
Worksheet retrieved from: https://ateachingmommy.com/water-cycle-free-printable/

Terminology Review; Read Aloud Book - The following Read-aloud book introduces many weather words. It could be used as an introduction or a review of the lesson’s terminology. Before the video begins, promote active listening by having them focus on the following terms and concepts as they watch the video. The teacher may want to put these questions on the chalkboard. Ask students to write down some new weather words they saw in the video. See: Weather Words by Gail Gibbons - Weather Read Aloud - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wVU31D2EmEE (7:53 min)
1. What weather vocabulary did you hear in the video? (Sunny, rainy, humidity, temperature, air pressure, moisture, wind, dew, water vapor, cumulous clouds, sirius clouds, stratus clouds)
2. What types of weather was seen? (rainy, cloudy, windy, stormy)
3. What is a definition of the word weather? (condition of the atmosphere at any given time)
4. What is the difference between weather and climate? (weather refers to short-term changes in the atmosphere, climate refers to atmospheric changes over longer periods of time)

Comprehension of Terminology - Place the following words in the proper blank in the sentences below. Precipitation/sun/condensation/evaporation/clouds 1. When water changes into water vapor and moves into the air, it is called ____. 2. When water vapor condenses in the atmosphere, it forms ____. 3. What happens after the clouds fill up with water vapor and become very heavy? 4. Energy for the water cycle on Earth comes from ____.
What is the change of a gas to a liquid called?
Create a Journal That Tracks Weather and Creates Data Tables and/or Graphs (such as histograms)

Assign students to create a journal about their observations of the different weather patterns observed for a week using drawings and descriptions of the weather. They should also include (temperature, wind direction, rainfall data)
Encourage them to use new words they have learned.

Ask students to measure and record in a data table, the rainfall in their school yard using a homemade rain gauge. Each day they should empty the gauge so that it monitors the daily rainfall rather than the accumulated rain.
At the end of the week, students use the data collected to plot a bar graph like the one shown below.

Answer the following questions using the information plotted on the graph. 1. On which day did they measure the most rainfall? 2. On which day did they measure the least rainfall? 3. What was the average rainfall for the week?
4. If you left the water in the rain gauge through the week, how might evaporation in the sunlight change your data? (loss of water on the sunniest days affects the correct daily amount)
Matching the Forecasting Tool to the Desired Information
Worksheet below retrieved rom: Grade 3: Match The Tools With The Correct Names Worksheet (worksheetzone.org)

Extended Learning: Evaporation
Evaporation rates impact the water cycle and therefore weather. The liquid water molecules require energy to become water gas molecules (vapor). Answer the following true/false questions about this system.
True or False:
1. Most evaporation took place on the hottest day. (T)
2. The least evaporation happened on the day with the lowest temperature (T).
3. The weather does not affect how fast water evaporates. (F)
4. High atmospheric pressure days make evaporation easier (F)
5. Low atmospheric pressure days often result in rainy weather. (T)

Preparing for Bad Weather Events
When we get a forecast of bad weather approaching our region, what do you see in these pictures below that we could prepare for?

Constructing a Warning Poster
Hold classroom discussions about safety during different weather conditions. Encourage students to share their experiences, questions, and ideas on how to stay safe during storms, extreme heat, cold weather, etc.
In groups have students prepare an information poster suggesting preparations.
It may take the form similar to the example below:
Retrieved from: https://www.pinterest.com/pin/606860118534168950/
inclusive_learning_strategies: Importance of Weather Knowledge - Teacher-led Discussion: When you hear the word “weather”, what does it mean to you? (sunny, rainy, cloudy, windy etc.)

Students, have you ever heard the words “weather forecasting”. It means to predict or make a good guess at what the weather might be in the future. The people who do this job of forecasting weather are called Meteorologists. Key questions to ask: 1) Why is important in our community to pay attention to weather data and weather forecasting/predictions? (prepare for good weather- clothing, picnics; prepare for bad weather- storms, rainy season, hurricanes etc.) 2) Which occupations in our community are often most reliant on good forecasting of weather? (fishers, farmers, drivers, tour guides, pilots) See more examples here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LHINAlqxD6M (5:26 mins)
Review of the Water Cycle - Weather depends primarily on the water cycle. In grade 2 you learned about how water on earth is in a cycle. Some steps we should review:

1) The liquid water molecules in lakes, ponds and rivers get enough heat energy from the sun that they begin to move further apart and become water in the gas form we call water vapor. This process is called evaporation. We say that the air pressure from the weight of the atmosphere above the water, affects the evaporation process. High pressure systems slow evaporation because water molecules can not easily jump into the air, whereas low air pressure allows the heat-energized molecules to jump into a vapor (gas) state quite easily. Air pressure is a result of the molecular collisions of the atmospheric gases above us and is measured with a barometer.

2) The water in gas form (vapor) rises into the sky, and as it cools in the higher atmosphere it loses energy, particles get close again and the water as gas (vapor) turns back to water as a liquid. We call that process condensation.
3) The liquid water molecules gather in clouds until they become so heavy, they fall to the earth again as rain. We call that process precipitation.
4) When the air is heavy with moisture, we say that it is humid.

Measuring Data Related to the Weather: In order to forecast or measure the impacts of weather, we often use special devices to measure wind direction, rainfall quantities and temperature. These measurements help to give us a picture of the usual weather in our region. This is called the climate.
Pupils will go to the following websites following instructions on how to make and use the various instruments to measure different weather conditions and explain how it is used.
Construction of Weather Instruments: How To Make a Rain Gauge Summer STEM Project: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rh18GMwwV4M (2:58 mins)
How to make an anemometer (wind speed meter) https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Af0LB3abBsk&t=23s (2:14 mins)
Make a Thermometer - STEM Activity https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rtsq-l0AxbY (1:54 mins)
Materials: Clear drinking straw, Permanent markers, Ruler, Small bottle, Rubbing alcohol, Food coloring, Modeling clay, Dropper

Field Trip /visit to Local Weather Station: Field trip to the weather station to observe the different weather instruments that are used. Pupils browse or look at the National Weather website of their island to learn how the meteorologist forecasts the weather.
How Do We Forecast Based on Cloud Types? Clouds have many overlapping categories but if we focus on three we can begin to forecast weather. Cumulous- puffy, wispy vertical movement > fair weather
Cirrus- high clouds > fair weather
Stratus- low clouds- contain water/ice > poor rainy weather
Cloud pictures retrieved from: https://www.instructables.com/id/Predicting-Weather-with-Clouds/

Setting Up Our Own Weather Station
After building their own weather measuring devices, the teacher should supply the students with a classroom chart table where each day (over a 1 month period) students measure and record the outside temperature, the wind direction (from which direction does it originate) and the rainfall.

Interpreting Weather Data
Activity One: Planning a Holiday
Tell students they are planning a holiday to London, England. Present to them, the weather graph below and pose the following questions:
a) If you were wanting to go to London at the warmest time of year, what would be a good month to choose? (July)
b) If you were hoping to see snow in London, what months would give the best combination of temperature and precipitation? (December/January: lowest temperature and highest precipitation)
Data retrieved from: https://mceegeogblog.wordpress.com/2012/11/30/london-climate-graph/

Activity Two: Precipitation Data
Give Students the Precipitation Table for Lima, Peru, below and ask the following questions:
a) What is the total rainfall for the year in Lima, Peru? (6.4 mm)
b) What are the driest two months in Lima? (Nov/Apr)
c) What is the average of the wettest three months in Lima? (1.0 + 1.5+ 0.8)/3= 1.1mm
Data retrieved from: https://www.lima.climatemps.com/precipitation.php

Activity Three: Wind in the Caribbean
Provide the following table to students regarding the wind in Saint Lucia for a particular day (where the time in the table is a 24 hour clock- i.e. 02= 2AM & 23= 11PM)

a) What was the average wind speed for the day? (6.11 m/s)
b) What direction did the wind blow from most of the day as represented in the table? (from the south east)
c) In terms of wind gusts, what time of the day would a sailor need to be most careful? (20 hrs or 8 pm)
Data retrieved from: https://windy.app/forecast2/spot/488863/St+Lucia

Where Did the Rain Water Go?
Students we know that on many Caribbean islands rain water is collected for use in the home. Philip had put a plastic bottle out in his front yard and noticed the first day it collected 2 mL of rain water. The second day his bottle had water up to the 5 mL mark. How much rain fell the second day? (5 mL- 2 mL = 3 mL)

On the third day it was bright and sunny and he noticed, at the end of the day, the bottle had water measuring up to the 3.5 mL mark. Where did the water go? (it got enough heat energy from the sun to leap into the sky as water vapor (gas)

How much water had Philip lost? (5mL – 3.5 mL = 1.5 mL)

If Philip wants to keep all the water he collects, how can he prevent it from evaporating? (place the bottle in a shady spot where it doesn’t get hot.)

Planning Our Lives Around Weather Forecasting:
Advantages and Disadvantages of weather conditions

Teachers should have a discussion with students around the following questions:

a) Why is rainy weather good or bad for us? (Good-Washing and cooking) (Bad- Excess rainfall causes flooding, landslides, transportation services e.g., bikers, open vehicles)

b) What are some of the activities we can and cannot do during the rainy weather? (planting crops, going to the beach)

c) How do we prepare for rainy weather? (Umbrellas, coats, clear drains to prevent flooding, repair roofs, collect water in barrels and drums)

d) How do we harvest rainwater for drinking and for irrigation? (barrels, eavestroughs)

Severe Weather Conditions
Pupils are presented with these questions to answer as they watch the following Read Aloud on Severe Weather: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MBn-Sdqu66A (3:33 mins)
1. What is severe weather? (when calm weather turns violent)
2. What type of severe weather was seen in the video? (thunder, lightning, tornado, flood, hurricane, blizzard)
3. What damage can a tornado cause? (It can rip apart houses and move trees.)
4. What damage can a hurricane cause? (It can damage houses and power lines.)
5. How can flooding affect us? (It covers the roads, fill up basements and houses.)
6. What is a blizzard? (A severe snow storm, with strong blowing wind.)
Teacher-Student Discussion: 1) How do we prepare for severe weather conditions?
2) Why is it important that we are prepared for extreme weather conditions? (We need to prepare, so that people and animals can be safe.)
3) How can we prepare for a hurricane? (By moving to a safe area, listening to the weather report, collecting food, water, medicines, first aid kit etc)

Climatic zones: polar/frigid, temperate, tropical/torrid
North /South pole
Temperature

Identify and name the sun, earth and the moon as parts of the solar system. (ST 1 ESS SS 2)
Explain, what is weather. (ST ESS EW 1)
Describe how temperature affects weather (ST ESS EW 8)
Explain the role of the sun as the main provider of heat and light on the earth. (ST 3 PS EN 2)
Identify and discuss some of the unintended consequences of using fuels for transport and production of materials for commerce. (e.g. pollution). (ST 3 PS EN 5)
Sustainable use of resources would help to minimize pollution and destruction of the environment. (ST 3 STSE 1)
Describe and discuss how burning can cause air pollution. (ST 6 ESS ER 4)
Define the terms: rotate, revolve, planet, star, satellite.(ST 3 ESS SS 2)

Explain how the tilt of the earth on its rotational axis can cause the sun’s rays to be direct and indirect for different parts of the earth and therefore lead to temperature changes on the surface (seasonal changes)
Demonstrate an understanding that the earth is divided into three major climatic zones.
Discuss the characteristics of each type of climate.
Compare the different types of climates.
Identify the plants and animals that can be found in each climatic zone.
Explain how people adapt to live in the different climatic zones.
Explain in simplified terms the process of global warming.
Observe different types of weather --rainy, sunny, cloudy windy. (ST 2 ESS EW 1)
Use a thermometer to measure temperature, explaining how the device works. (ST 3 ESS EW 9)
Identify types of weather conditions by examining weather charts. (ST 3 ESS EW 10)
Compare direct and indirect light using a flashlight (torch) to show how the sun’s energy can be spread out if it shines on the earth at an angle.
Interpret models of the earth and sun and how they interact in the universe. (daily rotation, yearly orbit, earth’s axis & tilt)
Predict weather patterns. (ST 2 ESS EW 4)
Analyse weather events.
Use the weather cycle to explain how/why humidity occurs.
Design and construct a project to learn how we can reduce climate change.
Develop appreciation for Science by recognising how climate affects our daily lives.
Willingly participate in activity to identify the climate of different regions of the world.
Recognise the effects of climate change in a region of the world and take the necessary actions to reduce the effects of climate change.
When undertaking classroom activities, display sensitivity to and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.
Consider the dilemma of abundant fossil fuel resources and the damage burning these will inflict to our environment.

Visitors to Other Climates: Play a game called “Pack my Bag”. Children describe to their peers what clothing they would pack in a suitcase to visit another part of the world. Their peers have to guess which climatic zone they are likely to be going to.
Define the terms: weather events, hurricane, typhoon, tornado, drought, erosion, landslides, land-borne floods, sea-borne floods, tsunami, pitch roof vs. flat roof, gable roof vs. hip roof, levee
Discuss how the various weather patterns affect their activities, the type of clothes they wear, and ways of dealing with various weather types. (ST 1 ESS EW 4)

Design Challenge: Engineers design solutions to solve real problems. You are going to do an experiment to decide how to best plug a hole in the retaining wall that protects your house from the overflowing brook beside your property.

To model this real-world problem, you will need the following items: two Styrofoam cups, a liter container of water, sample blocking agents (a sheet of paper towel, a piece of kitchen sponge, a piece of polyester fill or fabric), a measuring cylinder or graduated cup, a timer. Procedure: 1) use a pencil to poke a hole in the bottom of one of the Styrofoam cups. 2) place a small piece of paper towel over the hole on the inside of the cup. 3) place this cup with the hole inside the cup without a hole. 4) have your friend time 30 seconds while you ½ fill the inside cup with water. 5) withdraw the inside cup from the outside cup and measure how much water went through the paper towel-blocked hole in 30 seconds. 6) Repeat steps 1-5 with the kitchen sponge and then the polyester fill/fabric. 7) Make a data table of material and volume to compare which of your three samples was most effective at blocking the hole. i.e., the least amount of water went through to the second Styrofoam cup
Engineers would use a range of materials to attempt the very same experiment on a retaining wall that protected our homes from flooding! To solve problems, they use the design loop.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Introductory Discussion: Why is the daily outside temperature so consistent in the Caribbean? Students, you live in a part of the world near the equator (the imaginary line that divides evenly the earth between the south/north poles), that has very consistent temperatures throughout the year. Example: Dominica temperatures

You may have friends or relatives or seen movies that depict temperatures in the north and south that have a much bigger range. In fact, that range may be so large that they experience very cold temperatures and snowfall!
My question for you is why does that happen?
It is a common misconception that in the summer we are closer to the sun than the winter so distance isn't the reason for temperature change over the seasons.

Look at this picture of the earth as it faces the sun. What we notice right away is that the earth rotates on an axis that is tilted (24 hours per rotation-one day). That means that as the earth orbits around the sun (once per year), that the sun’s energy is approaching different places on the earth at a different angle.

Imagine you had a torch (flashlight) and you decided to shine it on a table at different angles; it can make circles or ovals (try it!). Below is a picture of the sun’s rays hitting the earth at different angles. In this picture from the Northern hemisphere (the half of the earth above the equator), you can see that the energy of the sun is spread out much more in December so people at that location would get less heat energy at any one spot compared to someone in June of that year where the light energy is more concentrated and shines more directly on that location.
Direct vs. Indirect Light Using A Flashlight

Retrieved from: https://physics.weber.edu/schroeder/ua/SunAndSeasons.html But, if you look back at our first picture, you will see that sun shines directly on the equator (circle) the entire year, so we get all the energy all the time. Anybody the lives in the Southern Hemisphere (the half of the earth below the equator) or the Northern Hemisphere (above the equator) will experience periods of the year where the sun’s light is direct and indirect (on an angle). This means less heat energy when the light is indirect and therefore colder temperatures!

Low pressure means evaporation happens easily and condensation later forms clouds that can drop rain) Extended learning: when pressure is low and temperatures are high, there is a lot of water evaporating and it places significant water in the air. In these instances, we would say there is high humidity. These days can be quite uncomfortable in the Caribbean!

High pressure means that water molecules do not have enough energy to form water vapor. Evaporation from lakes and rivers is less likely and therefore rainfall from condensation-is less likely-good weather!

Climate change and global warming
There is evidence that the earth overall has higher temperatures. This has been referred to as “global warming” and is caused by something called the greenhouse effect. (The greenhouse effect is defined as when the Earth's atmosphere becomes thick with gases and substances which trap the sun's radiation, as it reflects off the earth and back upwards. making the Earth warmer). One of the greenhouse gases is Carbon dioxide, a gas produced when we burn fuels in our stoves or cars. Everyday functions of the body like digesting your food, moving your muscles or even just thinking, need oxygen. When these processes happen, a gas called carbon dioxide is produced as a waste product. The job of your lungs is to provide your body with oxygen and to get rid of the waste gas, carbon dioxide. There are also gases such as water vapor, methane and ozone that trap the heat from the sun.
Notice in the picture how the radiant heat from the sun is reflected by gases down to the surface. This increase in surface temperature can kill plants and animals.

Using a Simulation to Show Surface Temperature
Use the following simulation to show children what happens in this process by increasing the clouds and watching what happens to the reflected energy from the sun.
See: https://phet.colorado.edu/en/simulation/legacy/greenhouse
Discuss with students what citizens can do to prevent global warming. (e.g. burn less fossil fuels, produce less gas/smoke from burning in cars and factories)

What are Weather Events in the Caribbean? Students, I read in the newspaper the other day that “weather events” have caused millions of dollars of damage in the from of “environmental destruction” in the Caribbean. If we break down the words environmental destruction, it must mean that somehow our environment (the world around us) is destroyed or changed in some way.. We are going to look at ways that can happen. What do you suppose they meant by the terms “weather event”?
When we think of normal weather patterns that contribute to our climate they include rain, wind, and temperature. But an event means something more drastic or surprising.
When winds are more than usual, what do we call them? (storms, hurricanes, typhoons, tornados)
Typhoon definition: a tropical storm in the region of the Indian or western Pacific oceans.
Tornado definition: a mobile formation of rotating winds having the appearance of a funnel-shaped cloud and advancing beneath a large storm system.
When our rainfall is more than usual what do we often see? (overflow of rivers & streams, flooding)
When our rainfall is less than usual we may experience something called drought where the extreme lack of rain can cause crops to die.
With more wind and rain we also often see higher tides and erosion near water.
These could all be called weather events because they involve effects that go beyond the usual weather patterns in our communities.
Recognizing the Problem Created by Weather Events: Students, because we experience weather events in the Caribbean as a semi-regular occurrence, we can actually prepare to limit the damage caused.
I want to show you some pictures and ask you to brainstorm how we might prevent some of the damage that is implied in the picture. (seawalls, diverting water, reinforcing river banks, drain pipes)
